gmw_00001477.name: SDSS J1030+0524
gmw_00001477.overview: SDSS J1030+0524 is a luminous quasar at a redshift of approximately 6.3 [arXiv:1207.2155, arXiv:2009.00024] powered by a supermassive black hole [arXiv:0707.1662, arXiv:2009.00024]. Although its host galaxy is not directly detected [arXiv:2309.04614], PSF-subtracted imaging reveals extended, tidal-tail-like structures that suggest a recent galaxy merger [arXiv:2309.04614].

sources.summary: The paper uses HST narrow-band imaging of J1030+0524 and finds no extended component, setting host line-emission and UV-continuum limits and finding no nearby emitters or halos.

quotes.quote: The present study aims to detect extended emission around two z>6 quasars (for which suitable narrow-band filters exist), SDSS J103027.10+052455.0 z=6.309 ; hereafter, J1030+0524 fan01 and SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 z=6.419 ; hereafter, J1148+5251 fan03 . The unique angular resolution offered by the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) allows us to disentangle the unresolved quasar light from any extended emission. We use the new narrow-band imaging capabilities offered by the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3) in order to sample both the pure continuum (`OFF' images) redwards of and the + continuum (`ON' images). Through accurate modeling of the PSF and its uncertainties, we will be able to constrain the presence of extended emission around the target quasars.

Results for J1030+0524 The light profiles of J1030+0524 (Fig. fig_results ) do not show any extended component. The small light excesses in the OFF observations (of both OBs) at radii 0.5'' are due to cosmic ray residuals which are observed in different positions in the two OBs (see Figure fig_ima ). We can set an upper limit to the magnitude of the extended component by comparing the observed profile with the ones simulated using GALFIT . We focus on the 0.2''-0.8'' scale, i.e., where the deviations from a PSF are expected to be significant but the signal is still high and the PSF models are still reliable. Given the uncertainties in the observed light profiles and in the PSF model, a host of 21 mag, both for the line and the continuum, is ruled out at 2- .

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSSp J103027.10+052455.0 as a z=6.28 quasar whose spectrum shows strong Ly absorption and possible Gunn-Peterson trough absorption near reionization.

quotes.quote: We describe the discovery of three new quasars, SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 ( z=5.82 ), SDSSp J130608.26+035626.3 ( z=5.99 ) and SDSSp J103027.10+052455.0 ( z=6.28 ). The quasar SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 is a radio source with flux of 1.1 mJy at 20 cm. The spectra of all three quasars show strong and broad Ly +NV emission lines, and very strong Ly forest absorption, with a mean continuum decrement D_A > 0.90 .

The three new quasars, with redshifts of 5.82, 5.99 and 6.28, are the highest-redshift objects with spectral confirmation known to date. The spectra of the new quasars show very strong Ly absorption blueward of the Ly emission, with more than 90 of the flux absorbed. The amount of absorption increases rapidly toward z_ abs 6 . For the z=6.28 quasar SDSS 1030+0524, the flux is consistent with zero in a region of 300 \ immediately blueward of Ly emission. It suggests a tentative detection of a complete Gunn-Peterson trough, indicating that at z 6 the universe is close to the reionization epoch.
The black hole masses of these quasars are probably several times 10^9 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper presents deep Chandra observations of the z=6.31 quasar, finding a harder, fainter spectrum than earlier XMM-Newton data and nearby diffuse X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: We present the results from a 500 ks Chandra observation of the z=6.31 QSO SDSS J1030+0524. This is the deepest X-ray observation to date of a z 6 QSO. The QSO is detected with a total of 125 net counts in the full ( 0.5-7 keV) band and its spectrum can be modeled by a single power-law model with photon index of = 1.81 0.18 and full band flux of f=3.95 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 . When compared with the data obtained by XMM- Newton in 2003, our Chandra observation in 2017 shows a harder ( 0.6 ) spectrum and a 2.5 times fainter flux. Such a variation, in a timespan of 2 yrs rest-frame, is unexpected for such a luminous QSO powered by a > 10^9 M_ black hole.

We also report the discovery of diffuse X-ray emission that extends for 30"x20" southward the QSO with a signal-to-noise ratio of 6, hardness ratio of HR=0.03_ 0.25 ^ +0.20 , and soft band flux of f_ 0.5-2 keV = 1.1_ 0.3 ^ +0.3 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , that is not associated to a group or cluster of galaxies. We discuss two possible explanations for the extended emission, which may be either associated with the radio lobe of a nearby, foreground radio galaxy (at z 1-2 ), or ascribed to the feedback from the QSO itself acting on its surrounding environment, as proposed by simulations of early black hole formation.

sources.summary: The paper studies the z=6.28 quasar SDSS J1030+0524 field, adding deep Y and J imaging and reinforcing evidence for an asymmetric overdensity of z~6 Lyman-break galaxies.

quotes.quote: The field around the z=6.28 quasar SDSSJ1030+0524 (J1030) is unique for multi-band coverage and represents an excellent data legacy for studying the environment around a primordial SMBH. In this paper we present wide-area ( 25' 25' ), Y- and J-band imaging of the J1030 field obtained with the near infrared camera WIRCam at the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope (CFHT).

We selected 21 robust high-z candidates in the J1030 field with photometric redshift z 6 and colors i-z 1.3. We found a significant asymmetry in the distribution of the high-z galaxies in J1030, supporting the existence of a coherent large-scale structure around the quasar.

Based on these numbers, we estimate that the J1030 field features an over-density of z 6 LBGs equal to =2.4 (defined as = _0 1, where _0 is the expected number counts, see M14 for details). We then confirm and reinforce the over-density of LBGs found in the J1030 field in M14 ( =2.0 at 3.3 ), where we compared the number of i-band dropouts in our field with that measured over the Subaru/XMM Deep Survey after applying the same optical color selection ( i-z>1.4 ) and after accounting for the different images depths and photometric systems.

sources.summary: The paper centers on SDSS J1030+0524, a z=6.31 luminous quasar powered by a billion-solar-mass black hole and hosted in a large-scale galaxy overdensity.

quotes.quote: We report on the spectroscopic confirmation of a large scale structure around the luminous, =6.31 QSO SDSS J1030+0524, that is powered by a billion solar mass black hole. The structure is populated by at least six members, four Lyman Break Galaxies (LBGs) and two Lyman Alpha Emitters (LAEs). The four LBGs have been identified among a sample of 21 i-band dropouts with z AB < 25.5 selected up to projected separations of 5 physical Mpc (15 arcmin) from the QSO. Their redshifts have been determined through up to 8hr-long multi-object spectroscopic observations at 8-10m class telescopes. The two LAEs have been identified in a 6hr VLT/MUSE observation centered on the QSO.
Among the 300 QSOs discovered so far at z > 5.7 2016ApJS..227...11B , SDSS J1030+0524 (hereafter J1030) is hosted in one of the most convincing large-scale galaxy overdensities.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J103027.10+052455.0 as a z=6.28 SDSS quasar target and reports a definite z_abs=5.7238 absorption doublet plus a possible second doublet in its ISAAC spectrum.

quotes.quote: We targeted two of the highest redshift QSOs discovered by the Sloan Digital Sky Survey: SDSS J103027.10+052455.0 ( z_ em =6.28 , J=18.87 , abbreviated to J1030+0524) and SDSS J130608.26+035626.3 ( z_ em =5.99 , J=18.77 , abbreviated to J1306+0356) over the wavelength range 9880 to 10800 .

In the case of J1030+0524, the total exposure time was 11 hours at 10110 \ and 10 hours at 10570 . For the latter object, two additional hours were spent unintentionally at an intermediate setting centred at 10330 ; however, these data turned out to be useful too as they provide an independent confirmation of the reality of the strong \ system at z_ abs = 5.7238 which is detected in both wavelength settings even though it was recorded at different physical locations on the detector.

However, two \ doublets, one definite and one possible, were flagged in the second spectrum of J1030+0524 which extends from 10340 to 10800 . As already explained, the first doublet, at _ obs = 10409.8, 10427.1 , is also present in the spectrum of J1030+0524 centred at _ = 10330 , providing additional confirmation that it is indeed real absorption rather than a detector defect or an artifact of the data processing. The two \ doublets are shown in Figure fig:spec1 after normalisation by the underlying QSO continuum.

sources.summary: The paper adopts near-zone measurements for quasar J1030+0524, compares its Ly-alpha and Ly-beta near-zones, and notes that scatter prevents firm neutral-fraction conclusions for the individual system.

quotes.quote: The size of near-zone around J1030+0524 they quote was increased to correspond to the size of the slightly larger near-zone observed around this quasar. We have instead adopted R_ = 4.55 1.65 , with an error bar based on an uncertainty of z = 0.03 in the redshift of J1030+0524 ( White03 ).

The filled circle with error bars show the observationally determined size of the and near-zones around J1030+0524 taken from White et al. (2003). We have assumed an uncertainty in the quasar redshift of z = 0.03 . Its location close to the R_ = R_ line appears to suggest that f_ HI 10^ 2 . However, the large scatter in near-zone size makes it very difficult to come to definite conclusions for individual systems.

sources.summary: The paper treats J1030+0524 as the most distant QSO in the sample, comparing published redshifts, Fe II/Mg II ratios, and a black-hole mass estimate of 1.4-3.2 billion solar masses.

quotes.quote: J1030+0524 The most distant QSO of our sample, at z = 6.28 was observed in the NIR by mai03 and pen02 . mai03 , again, find a much higher ratio of 8.7 2.5 than ours of 2.1 0.5, which, however, is consistent with the value of 2.1 1.1 derived by fre03 from a NICMOS spectrum sampling rest--frame wavelengths between 1600 and 2700 . The ratio derived by us is also consistent with the ratio of 0.99^ +1.86 _ 0.99 reported by iwa04 .

For J1030+0524, hai02 predict a minimum BH mass of 10^9 M_ based on the flux distribution of the \ emission alone and assuming it is not gravitationally lensed. The BH mass derived by us for this quasar is in the range 1.4-3.2 10^9 M_ , consistent with the prediction by hai02 .

sources.summary: The paper searches for X-ray lobes around SDSS J1030+0524, finds only likely confused nearby structures, adopts the ULAS-like upper limit, and notes no 1.4 GHz radio detection.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1030+0524 Farrah et al (2004) discuss SDSS J1030+0524 and its XMM data, which has about 75 ks of useable data. The X-ray image is shown in Fig. 1 (lower) and shows some structures close to the quasar which are most probably due to weak confused sources. We adopt the same upper limit to that for ULAS J1120+0641, given the similarities in XMM exposure time. The source was undetected in the radio band at 1.4 GHz by Petric et al (2003). The arcmin size, double radio source one arcmin S of the quasar shown in their 1.4GHz radio map is marginally coincident with several weak X-ray sources.

sources.summary: The paper reports a formal host non-detection for J1030+0524 but finds extended PSF-subtracted emission with tidal-tail-like features suggesting a recent merger.

quotes.quote: J1030+0524 (z=6.304) was initially reported by fan01 . The image fitting process returns a non-detection of the quasar host galaxy according to the criteria in Section sec:image:fitting . Nevertheless, the PSF-subtracted images clearly show extended emissions around the quasar, which have consistent shapes in all three bands. These features look like tidal tails and suggest a recent galaxy merging event. Note that we only got the data from three visits for J1030+0524 by the time of writing this paper.

sources.summary: The paper cites SDSS 1030+0524 as a z=6.28 QSO whose ionized region implies a 20 Myr lifetime, little lensing or beaming, and a roughly 2e9 solar-mass black hole.

quotes.quote: Haiman Cen (2002; see also Haiman Loeb 2001) applied this concept to the z = 6.28 QSO SDSS 1030+0524. They used the expected density distribution of the IGM with a hydrodynamic simulation (Cen McDonald 2002) and found that a quasar lifetime of 2 10^7 years and an H II region 4.5 Mpc (proper) radius produced a good match to the observed flux in SDSS 1030+524 blueward of . The masses of the black holes powering QSOs such as this one and others with z > 6 provide some additional information on the early evolution of QSOs. For example, one of the key conclusions of Haiman Cen (2002) was that the z = 6.28 QSO was not significantly lensed or beamed. Therefore if the QSO is shining at or below the Eddington rate, the mass of the central black hole is on order 2 10^9 .

sources.summary: The paper presents deep XMM-Newton spectroscopy of the z=6.30 quasar, finding a normal radio-quiet QSO X-ray spectrum, little absorption, and possible FeK emission.

quotes.quote: We present a deep XMM-Newton observation of the z=6.30 QSO SDSS J1030+0524, the second most distant quasar currently known. The data contain sufficient counts for spectral analysis, demonstrating the ability of XMM-Newton to measure X-ray spectral shapes of z 6 QSOs with integration times 100 ks. The X-ray spectrum is well fit by a power law with index =2.12 0.11 , an optical-X-ray spectral slope of _ ox =-1.80 , and no absorption excess to the Galactic value, though our data are also consistent with a power law index in the range 2.02 < < 2.5 and excess absorption in the range 0 < N_ H (cm^ 2 ) < 8 10^ 22 . There is also a possible detection ( 2 ) of FeK emission. The X-ray properties of this QSO are, overall, similar to those of lower-redshift radio-quiet QSOs.

sources.summary: The paper images the z=6.30 quasar field, finds only faint foreground galaxies several arcseconds away, and notes an overdensity of red objects reported in deeper ACS data.

quotes.quote: We present observations for three quasars: SDSS J103027.10+052455.0 (SDSS J1030+0524; z=6.30 ), SDSS J104845.05+463718.3 (SDSS J1048+4637; z=6.20 ) and SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 (SDSS J1148+5251; z=6.42 ). Spurred on by this discovery, this group observed two more high-redshift quasar fields (SDSS J1030+0524 at z=6.30 and SDSS J1306+0356 at z=5.99 ), but failed to find galaxies projected closer than 6 and 3 to these quasars, respectively (Yamada et al. 2003). One of these fields, SDSS J1030+0524, has been imaged before by Yamada et al. (2003), but our images are 1-2 mags deeper and have better resolution than those in Yamada et al. and Shioya et al.

For SDSS J1030+0524, the objects are all rather faint ( i'>26 ) and distant from the quasar ( 2.5 ). The single i'-z'>1.5 object we discovered in the field of SDSS J1030+0524 which is plotted on Fig. fig:colmags is also found by Stiavelli et al. and a high-resolution image of it is shown in the upper panel of their Fig. 2.

sources.summary: The paper imposes a larger minimum ionized region for J1030+0524, finds higher R_S and inferred luminosity than MH07, and recovers damping-wing lower limits.

quotes.quote: In particular, for J1030+0524, which has a Ly trough that lies at a significantly larger distance from the quasar than the Ly trough, we impose a minimum size for the ionized region to R > 52 Mpc, corresponding to the location of the Ly trough (as opposed to 40 Mpc in MH07, using the old redshift).

We also find significantly higher R_S values for both J1030+0524 and J1623+3112 compared to MH07, caused by the increase in the intrinsic redshifts of these two quasars. This also leads to a factor of 2-3 larger inferred quasar luminosities, which is not surprising: the material at the same observed wavelength is now farther from the quasar, so a larger intrinsic luminosity is needed to produce similar absorption. When we use the same Q_ KS statistic as MH07, we further recover very similar lower limits on , attributable to GP-damping wings for both J1030+0524 and J1623+3112.

sources.summary: The paper uses combined FIRE/X-Shooter spectra for J1030+0524 and flags it as a continuum-reconstruction exception, with predicted flux low around N 5 and a possible unusual C 3 profile.

quotes.quote: For three of the quasars in our sample, i.e. J1030+0524, J159--02, and J1120+0641, we use combined Magellan/FIRE and VLT/X-Shooter vernet_x-shooter_2011 spectra, while one other spectrum in our sample, i.e. J1148+5251, was observed with Keck/MOSFIRE mclean_design_2010,mclean_mosfire_2012 and Keck/ESI sheinis_esi_2002 .

Of particular interest might be the predicted continua of ULAS J1120+0641, J0252–0503, J1148+5251, and J1030+0524, as the damping wings of these objects have been studied using a variety of methods mortlock_luminous_2011,davies_predicting_2018,wang_significantly_2020,greig_igm_2022,greig_constraints_2019,schroeder_evidence_2013,mesinger_constraints_2007 , including QSANNdRA durovcikova_reionization_2020 .

A few notable exceptions are J159-02 and J1030+0524, where the predicted continuum seems to underestimate the transmitted flux around the N 5 line. The interesting thing to note is that in both of these cases, the SDSS nearest neighbor composite shown in fig:QSANNdRA_predictions also underpredicts the flux in this region -- this means that either the C 3 line that is masked by the region of telluric absorption is unusual in these two quasars, or this discrepancy shows a limitation of the training set of the neutral network.

sources.summary: The paper models the absorption spectrum of the z=6.28 quasar SDSS J1030+0524, using its Gunn-Peterson troughs and optical-depth thresholds to constrain neutral hydrogen near reionization.

quotes.quote: In particular, we modeled broad features of the and regions of the absorption spectrum of the z=6.28 quasar SDSS J1030+0524. The observational input to our analysis is the deepest available absorption spectrum of \ , shown in Figure fig:spectrum . The spectrum exhibits a strong Gunn-Peterson (GP) trough, with no detectable flux between wavelengths corresponding to redshifts 5.97 < z < 6.20 , as well as a somewhat narrower trough between 5.97< z < 6.18 , as shown in Figure fig:spectrum . The flux detection threshold in the and regions of this spectrum correspond to optical depths of 6.3 and 22.8 respectively For our purposes, these optical depths can be taken as rough estimates.

sources.summary: The paper presents J1030+0542 as a z~6 quasar in an average-density environment, with only two nearby emitting galaxies, no significant enhancement, and no ALMA-detected molecular-gas emission from its host.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, one of the targeted quasar fields around J1030+0542, shows only 2 emitting galaxies within the | v| 1000 km s^ 1 velocity interval from the quasar and therefore no galaxy enhancement compared to a blank field, i.e. \ (<2 cMpc )=7 8 .

While J0148+0600 with 47 emitting galaxies in its surroundings likely resides in one of the highest-density peaks, and traces one of the largest high-redshift proto-cluster reported to date, J1030+0542 on the other hand appears to reside in an average density region with no enhancement compared to a random field which is in contrast to the proto-cluster reported in this field by Mignoli2020 .

It is interesting to note that the \ emission arising from the molecular gas of the host galaxy of J1030+0542 was not detected with ALMA, indicating that the FIR luminosity of this quasar is significantly fainter than for most other z 6 quasars with similar rest-frame UV magnitudes Decarli2018 .

gmw_00001478.name: 2MASX J20323421 5628162
gmw_00001478.overview: 2MASX J20323421 5628162 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:2008.00660]. The galaxy features jets oriented in a northeast-southwest direction and is likely a foreground object rather than a cluster member [arXiv:2008.00660].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2MASX J20323421 5628162 as an FR-I radio galaxy with NE-SW jets, bright WISE magnitudes, no reported redshift, and likely foreground status rather than cluster membership.

quotes.quote: Source B is an FR-I radio galaxy associated with 2MASX J20323421 5628162 with jets oriented roughly NE--SW (the same orientation as the SE relic source). The host galaxy has no reported redshift, but has a lower magnitude than the surrounding galaxies in . The host galaxy has WISE Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer; wright_wide-field_2010 W1 ( W2 ) magnitudes of 13.597 0.025 ( 13.365 0.030 ) compared to the mean for the 31 cluster members of 15.8 1.0 ( 15.6 1.0 ). We do not consider that this galaxy should be significantly brighter than the cluster members, and given it is not near the centre of the cluster it is unlikely this is the BCG of . Given the difference in WISE magnitudes, we suggest this galaxy is not a cluster member.

gmw_00001479.name: member 8971
gmw_00001479.overview: Member 8971 is a cluster member galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lens [arXiv:2310.02310]. Multiple images of a background source are observed close to the galaxy, which has an estimated total mass of approximately 1.2 × 10^11 solar masses [arXiv:2310.02310].

sources.summary: The paper models member 8971 as the main lens galaxy near ID14 images, constraining its truncated mass profile, total mass, and velocity dispersion while comparing SISt and SIE descriptions.

quotes.quote: All of the multiple images of the source ID14 are observed close to the cluster member 8971 (hereafter member 8971), at an average angular distance of 1.07'' : we thus focused on constraining its truncation radius.

In bergamini22 , member 8971 was not included in the scaling relations adopted to describe the remaining cluster members, and it was modelled separately as a truncated dPIE profile. The values of the parameters of its total mass distribution are reported in Table id14b23 , with an uncertainty provided by the MCMC sampling of their marginalised posterior probability distribution. The ellipticity of the halo converges to the upper limit of its prior, indicating that it is poorly constrained and perhaps compensates for some unaccounted shear.

Unlike in bergamini22 , where LensTool struggled to recover r_t and the ellipticity of member 8971 at the same time, all the parameters of the SISt and SIE models are well constrained, with a low uncertainty. Their values are reported in Table id14g23 . In the case of the SISt mass profile, the total mass can be obtained as M = ^2 r_t G . We can thus estimate a total mass value of M=1.2^ +0.3 _ 0.1 10^ 11 M_ for 8971.

gmw_00001480.name: member 8785
gmw_00001480.overview: The galaxy member 8785 is a cluster member that acts as a galaxy-scale gravitational lens [arXiv:2310.02310]. It is situated close to six multiple images of the lensed source ID16 [arXiv:2310.02310].

sources.summary: The paper uses member 8785 as a galaxy-scale lens near six ID16 images, constraining its truncation radius and finding a lower total mass than scaling-relation models predicted.

quotes.quote: All six of the multiple images of the source ID16 are observed close to the cluster member 8785 (hereafter member 8785), at an average angular distance of only 0.43'' from its centre. We thus focused on constraining the truncation radius of member 8785, and optimised the mass distribution of member 9129, which significantly influences the multiple-images configuration, as well. In bergamini22 , member 8785 was modelled using the scaling relations (Eqs. scaling1 and scaling2 ) leading to a velocity dispersion value = 83.3^ +2.7 _ 6.7 km s^ 1 and a truncation radius r_t = 0.8^ +0.2 _ 0.1 '' , resulting in a total mass M=2.1^ +0.3 _ 0.2 10^ 10 M_ .

We first described member 8785 as a SISt, and we studied an alternative SIE model. Again, we tested several models for member 9129, and we chose a SIS mass profile to avoid degeneracies between parameters. We ran the SISt and SIE models for each of the 100 cluster-scale total mass distributions extracted from the MCMCs.
Comparing our results for the SISt model with those from bergamini22 , we find lower values for and r_t , corresponding to a lower total mass value of M=1.0^ +0.2 _ 0.1 10^ 10 M_ .

gmw_00001481.name: member 3910
gmw_00001481.overview: Member 3910 is a galaxy located within the MACS J1206 cluster [arXiv:2310.02310]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens, with six multiple images of a background source observed in close proximity to its center [arXiv:2310.02310].

sources.summary: The paper models member 3910 as the main lens near six ID14 images in MACS J1206, deriving a larger truncation radius and total mass than earlier scaling-relation estimates.

quotes.quote: All six of the multiple images of the source ID14 are observed close to the cluster member 3910 (hereafter member 3910), at an average angular distance of 1.11'' from its centre: we thus focused on constraining its truncation radius.

Cluster member 2541, at a distance of 6.86'' from the centre of 3910, is the second brightest cluster galaxy, with a predicted total mass of 9.55 10^ 11 M_ in bergamini19 . Closer to 3910 is the low-mass ( M=1.97 10^ 9 M_ ) cluster member 3920. Due to its high total mass, we optimised the mass distribution of cluster member 2541 as well, whereas we kept the parameters describing 3920 fixed to the values predicted by cluster-scale modelling. Member 3910 was described by bergamini19 with the two scaling relations (Eqs. scaling1 and scaling2 ), leading to best-fit values of the velocity dispersion and of the truncation radius of = 136.6^ +7.7 _ 6.6 km s^ 1 and r_t = 0.53^ +0.09 _ 0.07 '' , for a total mass M=4.2^ +0.4 _ 0.4 10^ 10 M_ . Again, we first described 3910 as a SISt, and we tested an alternative SIE model.

In spite of having fewer free parameters, the SISt model allows for a more accurate reconstruction of the lensing observables, and all parameters are estimated with a low statistical uncertainty, as clear from Table id141206g23 . On the other hand, the SIE model cannot constrain well the value of the velocity dispersion of member 2540, which tends to the upper limit of our prior, and the predicted ellipticity of member 3910 is very high As such, the value of _ 3910 of the SISt model cannot be compared directly to that of the SIE model. , suggesting that it might be needed to compensate for the lack of truncation in the total mass profile. Comparing our SISt model with bergamini19 , we find lower values for and significantly higher values for r_t , for a total mass value of M=6.3^ +1.0 _ 1.1 10^ 10 M_ , higher than the best-fit value from bergamini19 .

gmw_00001482.name: NGC 4330
gmw_00001482.overview: NGC 4330 is an edge-on late-type spiral galaxy in the Virgo Cluster undergoing nearly face-on ram pressure stripping [arXiv:2112.15118]. This interaction has stripped approximately 80% of its atomic hydrogen [arXiv:2011.03437], creating H I, H-alpha, ultraviolet, and radio continuum tails [arXiv:2011.03437, arXiv:1304.1279].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4330 as one of three Virgo spirals with ram pressure stripping, disturbed molecular gas morphology and kinematics, upstream CO enhancement, and shrinking star-forming disks.

quotes.quote: We present 12CO (2--1) data of three Virgo spirals -- NGC 4330, NGC 4402 and NGC 4522 obtained using the Submillimeter Array. These three galaxies show clear evidence of ram pressure stripping due to the cluster medium as found in previous H i imaging studies.

At given sensitivity limits, we do not find any clear signs of molecular gas stripping. However, both its morphology and kinematics appear to be quite disturbed as those of H i . Morphological peculiarities present in the molecular and atomic gas are closely related with each other, suggesting that molecular gas can be also affected by strong ICM pressure even if it is not stripped.

CO is found to be modestly enhanced along the upstream sides in these galaxies, which may change the local star formation activity in the disc. Indeed, the distribution of H emission, a tracer of recent star formation, well coincides with that of the molecular gas, revealing enhancements near the local CO peak or along the CO compression. FUV and H share some properties in common, but FUV is always more extended than CO/H in the three galaxies, implying that the star-forming disc is rapidly shrinking as the molecular gas properties have changed.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4330 as a Virgo spiral undergoing ram-pressure stripping, with UV, H i, and H tails, quenched outer-disk star formation, and an H i deficiency indicating major atomic-gas loss.

quotes.quote: NGC 4330 is one of the Virgo galaxies whose UV emission distribution shows a tail structure. An associated tail structure is also observed in the H i and H emission distributions. Previous dynamical modeling showed that the galaxy is approaching the cluster center and is therefore undergoing increasing ram pressure stripping. Recent stellar population fitting of deep optical spectra together with multiband photometry lead to the determination of the time when star formation was quenched in the galactic disk.

NGC 4330 is one of the Virgo spiral galaxies with a long H i tail observed by Chung et al. (2007). It has a maximum rotation velocity of v_ rot 180 km s ^ 1 and a total H i mass of M_ HI =4.5 10^ 8 M _ (Chung et al. 2009). NGC 4330 is located at a projected distance of 2^ (600 kpc) from the cluster center, i.e. it is relatively close to M 87, and has a radial velocity of 400 km s ^ 1 with respect to the Virgo cluster mean. The H i deficiency of NGC 4330, which is defined as the logarithm of the ratio of the \ content of a field galaxy of same morphological type and diameter to the observed \ mass, is 0.8 (Chung et al. 2007), i.e. the galaxy has lost about 80 of its atomic hydrogen.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4330 as actively ram-pressure stripped, with truncated H I, gas and UV tails, enhanced molecular fraction on the windward side, and reduced extraplanar-gas star formation efficiency.

quotes.quote: NGC 4330 (Fig. fig:plot_n4330 ): the H i distribution is truncated within the optical disk to the northeast and has a low surface density tail to the southwest (Chung et al. 2009) without a stellar counterpart. No CO is detected in this gas tail (Vollmer et al. 2011). A UV tail is present in the southwest offset from the gas tail (Abramson et al. 2011). According to the dynamical model of Vollmer et al. (2011) the galaxy is moving to the north.

The molecular fraction is highest at this side of the galaxy. SFE_ tot decreases by a factor of 2 along the direction of the galaxy's minor axis from the northwest to the southeast. This gradient is also observed in the gas tail.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4330 as an edge-on Virgo late-type galaxy undergoing nearly face-on ram pressure stripping, using H-alpha imaging, Fabry-Perot kinematics, and simulations to trace its disturbed gas.

quotes.quote: Using the VESTIGE survey, a deep narrow-band H imaging survey of the Virgo cluster carried on at the CFHT with MegaCam, we have discovered a long and diffuse tail of ionised gas in the edge-on late-type galaxy NGC 4330. This peculiar feature witnesses an ongoing ram pressure stripping event able to remove the gas in the outer region of the disc. Tuned hydrodynamic simulations suggest that the ram pressure stripping event is occurring almost face-on, making NGC 4330 the ideal candidate to study the effects of the perturbation in the direction perpendicular to the disc plane.

We present here two new independent sets of Fabry-Perot observations ( R 10000) with the purpose of understanding the effects of ram pressure stripping process on the kinematics of the ionised gas. Despite their limited sensitivity to the diffuse gas emission, the data allowed us to measure the velocity and the velocity dispersion fields over the galaxy disc and in several features at the edges or outside the stellar disc formed after the ram pressure stripping event. We have constructed the position-velocity diagrams and the rotation curves of the galaxy using three different techniques. The data show, consistent with the hydrodynamic simulations, that the galaxy has an inner solid-body rotation up to 2.4 kpc, with non-circular streaming motions outwards the disc and in the several external features formed during the interaction of the galaxy with the surrounding intracluster medium.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4330 as a Virgo spiral undergoing pre-peak ram-pressure stripping, with bent leading-side emission, offset H I and UV tails, no polarized ridge, and northward motion through the intracluster medium.

quotes.quote: It has been shown that the Virgo spiral galaxy NGC 4330 shows signs of ongoing ram pressure stripping in multiple wavelengths: at the leading edge of the interaction, the H and dust extinction curve sharply out of the disk; on the trailing side, a long H /UV tail has been found which is located upwind of a long H i tail. We complete the multiwavelength study with IRAM 30m HERA CO(2-1) and VLA 6 cm radio continuum observations of NGC 4330. In contrast to other Virgo spiral galaxies affected by ram pressure stripping, NGC 4330 does not show an asymmetric ridge of polarized radio continuum emission.

From the H i , CO, H , UV, and radio continuum observations compared to the dynamical modeling the following overall picture arises: NGC 4330 moves approximately northward within the Virgo intracluster medium which is assumed to be static (see also Vollmer 2009). The current ram pressure acting on NGC 4330 is p_ rps 2500  cm ^ 3 (km s ^ 1 ) ^ 2 . NGC 4330's gas tail consists only of atomic hydrogen and the UV tail is upwind from the H i tail.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4330 undergoing ram-pressure stripping, with asymmetric CO, UV emission beyond the molecular gas, rapid outer-region quenching, and a model placing it about 100 Myr before peak stripping.

quotes.quote: Recently Lee17 presented high-resolution CO maps of \ revealing an asymmetric CO morphology which closely coincides with the location of the H emission. However, the UV emission is more extended than the molecular gas distribution, which suggests a recent and rapid quenching of the star formation activity occurred during the stripping of all the gaseous components from the outer regions of NGC4330. Vollmer12 presented a ram pressure stripping model of NGC4330 tailored to reproduce the observed morphology and extension of different stellar populations.

This model suggests that the stripping occurs almost face-on (the angle between the galaxy's disc and the wind direction is 75 ) and the peak of the stripping force has not yet been reached. The best fit to the observations is obtained 100 Myr before the peak of stripping is reached. This explains the significant star formation activity in the galaxy centre where the quenching has not yet been effective.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4330 as a ram-pressure-stripped edge-on galaxy with H I, UV, and radio continuum tails, distorted southern halo fields, and modeled motion toward the cluster center.

quotes.quote: NGC 4330 The edge-on galaxy NGC 4330 has a prominent H i and UV tail to the southwest (Chung et al. 2009, Abramson et al. 2011). The UV and H i morphologies are interpreted as signs of ongoing ram pressure stripping (Abramson et al. 2011). Murphy et al. (2009) found that the local radio/FIR ratio is lower than the global ratio at the northern border of the H i disk which they ascribe to ongoing ram pressure stripping. The 6 cm continuum emission is asymmetric with respect to the major axis with more emission to the southeast. A radio continuum tail is observed which is offset to the east from the H i tail.

sources.summary: NGC 4330 is described as a Virgo spiral with an upturn of dust and young stars, including a prominent dust plume likely shaped by ICM wind stripping.

quotes.quote: An upturn structure similar in some ways to the one in NGC 4522 has been identified in the Virgo Cluster spiral NGC 4330 Abramson11 , visible in dust and young stars curving downwind away from the major axis at a fairly steep angle. The most prominent feature of this upturn is a large, continuous, highly extincting dust plume extending 1.5 kpc downwind from the major axis. Some evidence was found for a concentration of blue stars along the outer edge of the dust plume, and it is possible that the galaxy contains a stellar upturn located radially outside of the upturn dust plume, although the ground-based data was inconclusive. Like the upturn in NGC 4522, the upturn in NGC 4330 also seems to be the result of the ICM wind pushing ISM out of the disk at gradually smaller radii. There are some key differences, such as the fact that the NGC 4330 upturn does not seem to have shallow and steep components or a separate stellar upturn.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4330 as a ram-pressure-stripped normal galaxy with FUV filaments, bluer halo color, little radial color change, and similar H I and UV morphology.

quotes.quote: Filaments are most easily visible in the FUV, and they are seen in most starburst galaxies (M82, NGC 253, NGC 3079, NGC 3628, NGC 4666, and NGC 5775), as well as two normal galaxies (NGC 4522, NGC 4330). The filaments in the latter two are associated with gas stripped by ram pressure in the Virgo cluster.
For galaxies where the S/N is reasonably high across the disk (NGC 55, NGC 891, NGC 4217, and NGC 4330), the color does not change much across with projected galactocentric radius.
The H 1 and UV morphology are similar for the galaxies undergoing ram-pressure stripping (NGC 134, NGC 4522, NGC 4330, and NGC 4388).

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 4330 in Virgo, likely on first infall, and explains that cirrus limits the northwest side while profiles probe the clearer southeast.

quotes.quote: NGC 4330 NGC 4330 is a member of the Virgo cluster, likely falling in for the first time abramson11 . Similar to NGC 3044, NGC 4330 is coincidentally located in a hole in the cirrus. Near the galaxy itself, cirrus contamination is worse northwest of the disc plane (where two bright stars prevent any investigation of the stellar halo anyway) while the southeast is clear. Our profiles and resulting analysis effectively probe the latter region.

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 4330 radio-bright and radio-deficit regions to stripped extraplanar gas and cosmic-ray transport with the interstellar medium under ram pressure.

quotes.quote: The one-sided radio-bright regions in NGC 4330 (Fig. fig:rcfir_spixx1c1_nice_2 ) and NGC 4522 (Fig. fig:rcfir_spixx1c1_nice_3 ) coincide with stripped extraplanar gas (Vollmer et al. 2006, 2012, 2021) and are thus due to CR transport together with the interstellar medium (ISM) via ram pressure.
These authors found significant radio-deficit regions in NGC 4254, NGC 4330, NGC 4402, and NGC 4522.

Based on our knowledge of the Virgo spiral galaxies, we identified the physical causes of radio-bright regions: CR advection transport via gravitational tides in M 51, CR transport via a galactic wind in NGC 4532, CR transport via ram pressure stripping in NGC 4330, and NGC 4522.

gmw_00001483.name: COOL J1323+0343
gmw_00001483.overview: COOL J1323+0343 is an early-type, quiescent galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 1.016 [arXiv:2203.11957, arXiv:2212.04731]. It is strongly lensed by a foreground galaxy cluster [arXiv:2203.11957] and exhibits a compact size with a very low star formation rate [arXiv:2203.11957].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes COOL J1323+0343 as a strongly lensed z~1 early-type galaxy, deriving its mass, low recent star formation, compact size, and source-plane morphology.

quotes.quote: We present COOL J1323+0343, an early-type galaxy at z = 1.0153 0.0006 , strongly lensed by a cluster of galaxies at z = 0.353 0.001 . This object was originally imaged by DECaLS and noted as a gravitational lens by COOL-LAMPS, a collaboration initiated to find strong-lensing systems in recent public optical imaging data, and confirmed with follow-up data. With ground-based grzH imaging and optical spectroscopy from the Las Campanas Observatory and the Nordic Optical Telescope, we derive a stellar mass, metallicity, and star-formation history from stellar-population synthesis modeling. The lens modeling implies a total magnification of 113.

The median remnant stellar mass in the source plane is M _* 10.63 M_ and the median star-formation rate in the source plane is SFR 1.55 10^ 3 M _ yr ^ 1 (log sSFR = -13.4 yr ^ 1 ) in the youngest two age bins (0-100 Myr), closest to the epoch of observation. Our measurements place below the characteristic mass of the stellar mass function, making it an especially compelling target that could help clarify how intermediate mass quiescent galaxies evolve. We reconstruct in the source plane and fit its light profile. This object is below the expected size-evolution of early-type galaxy at this mass with an effective radius _e 0.5 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 132304+034319 as a z=1.016 lensed quiescent galaxy with red arcs, no emission lines, old stellar age, and low Fe and Mg abundances.

quotes.quote: The two gravitationally lensed galaxies, 132304+034319 (hereafter ) at z=1.016 and 001424+004145 ( ) at z=1.374 , were identified as part of the \ survey Tran22 . The \ survey performed spectroscopic observations on strong gravitational lenses selected from the Dark Energy Survey and the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey to measure their redshifts. These lensed galaxy candidates were discovered using convolutional neural networks Jacobs19a, Jacobs19b and were notable for their red arcs. \ was also independently discovered by the COOL-LAMPS Surveys as COOL J1323+0343 Sukay22 in a visual search of the northern galactic cap portion of the southern DECaLS dataset. Neither galaxy displayed emission lines in the identification spectra from \ survey, indicating their quiescent nature.

We measured the age, [ Fe/H ] , and [ Mg/Fe ] of the two lensed galaxies using deep, rest-frame-optical spectra (S/N 40 ^ 1 ) obtained on the Keck I telescope. The ages of \ and \ are 5.6^ +0.8 _ 0.8 Gyr and 3.1^ +0.8 _ 0.3 Gyr, respectively, indicating that most of the stars in the galaxies were formed less than 2 Gyr after the Big Bang. Compared to nearby quiescent galaxies of similar masses, the lensed galaxies have lower [ Fe/H ] and [ Mg/H ] . Surprisingly, the two galaxies have comparable [ Mg/Fe ] to similar-mass galaxies at lower redshifts, despite their old ages.

gmw_00001484.name: host of GRB 110918A
gmw_00001484.overview: The host of GRB 110918A is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 1 [arXiv:1308.5520]. It is characterized as a dusty, metal-rich system with a high stellar mass for its redshift [arXiv:1308.5520].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 110918A host as an unusually massive, dusty, metal-rich GRB-selected galaxy whose properties challenge simple metallicity cutoffs for long GRB production.

quotes.quote: The subject of this work is the very energetic GRB 110918A ( E_ , iso =1.9 10^ 54 erg ), for which we measure one of the largest host-integrated metallicities, ever, and the highest stellar mass for z<1.9 . Host Galaxy The host of GRB 110918A was detected in 11 different filters ranging from the ultra-violet to 4.5 m, yielding a well-sampled photometric SED (see Fig. fig:_sedhost ).

We have derived estimates for the mass, metallicity and SFR of the host of GRB 110918A, which facilitates comparing this galaxy with respect to normal star forming galaxies through the fundamental metallicity relation . A dedicated follow-up campaign of the afterglow of GRB 110918A and its underlying host has revealed a unique system with the following properties: (i) one of the most massive galaxies selected by a GRB at z 1 , (ii) the first relatively unobscured afterglow that has been detected in a very massive and dusty host galaxy, (iii) one of the most metal-rich long GRB host galaxies found yet, (iv) no different to star forming galaxies selected through their own stellar light, when compared using the FMR, (v) opposes an anti-correlation between energy output of the GRB and environmental metallicity, and (vi) contradicts a cut-off for host galaxies of Z<0.5 Z_ .

gmw_00001485.name: J0751+1730
gmw_00001485.overview: J0751+1730 is classified as a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1309.2774]. It is unresolved at kiloparsec scales, but high-frequency observations suggest a slightly resolved, lobe-dominated structure with a steep radio spectrum [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0751+1730 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0751+1730. This source is unresolved at kiloparsec-scale with a total flux density of 10 mJy in NVSS maps. From VLA data archive (AB878) in B configuration, at 8 GHz, we obtained a slightly resolved image ( 1.4 with a HPBW = 0.7 ) which implies _ 1.4 ^ 8.4 0.9. It is undetected in our VLBA images but with an upper limit higher than NVSS flux density (for technical issues it was not observed in phase reference mode), for this reason we classify this source among the U sources, even if the steep spectrum from VLA data suggests the presence of a lobe dominated structure.

gmw_00001486.name: J0751+2913
gmw_00001486.overview: J0751+2913 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774]. The source is unresolved in FIRST radio images with a flux density of 9 mJy at 1.4 GHz, but it remains undetected at 15 GHz [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0751+2913 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0751+2913. This BL Lac is unresolved in FIRST images with flux density of 9 mJy. Not observed for technical reasons at 8 GHz, it is not detected at 15 GHz above 1.8 mJy beam ^ 1 . Because of the low flux density at 1.4 GHz we put it among undetermined objects.

gmw_00001487.name: J0754+3910
gmw_00001487.overview: J0754+3910 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1809.01173, arXiv:1309.2774] that exhibits a core-jet morphology at 8 GHz and appears point-like at 15 GHz [arXiv:1309.2774]. It is classified as Doppler-dominated but remains undetected in gamma rays [arXiv:1809.01173].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0754 + 3910 with marginal LAT TS=12, predicts possible future detection, and classifies it as Doppler-dominated but currently gamma-ray undetected with relatively low core dominance.

quotes.quote: For two sources the likelihood analysis results in a TS between 10 and 25: J0754 + 3910 (TS = 12) and J0810 + 4911 (TS = 11). Adding more data, these sources could be detected by Fermi LAT in the future.

On the other hand, three sources classified as DD for their radio properties (J0754 + 3910, J0903 + 4055, J1516 + 2918) are not detected in rays by LAT up to now. These three sources have a relatively low CD (7.6--8.0) with respect to the LAT-detected sources classified as DD (5.5--72.4). Considering that CD is a strong indication of Doppler beaming of the emission, this suggests that the DD sources not detected by LAT have a lower Doppler factor with respect to the LAT-detected ones.

In the cases of J0754 + 3910 and J0903 + 4055, the upper limits on the ray flux estimated over 8.5 years of LAT observations are compatible with the sensitivity limit estimated over 10 years of LAT observations, indicating that, accumulating more data, these sources may be detected by the LAT in the near future.

sources.summary: The paper gives J0754+3910 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0754+3910. This source shows a faint one-sided structure in NVSS images, but it appears unresolved in FIRST. In our 8 GHz VLBA map, it shows a core-jet morphology with a total flux density of 27.0 mJy. At 15 GHz, it appears point-like with a total flux density of 17.8 mJy. Bourda et al.

gmw_00001488.name: J0809+3455
gmw_00001488.overview: J0809+3455 is a BL Lacertae candidate [arXiv:1411.0361] classified as a non-extreme high-energy blazar [arXiv:1911.06680]. On parsec scales, it features a weak, steep-spectrum radio core with a one-sided jet extending to the south [arXiv:1411.0361, arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper describes B2 0806+35 as a BL-Lac candidate with kiloparsec and parsec-scale radio emission, a weak steep-spectrum core-jet structure, no detected polarization, and blueshifted H I absorption.

quotes.quote: B2 0806+35 This source shows radio emission both on the kpc and pc scale. It has been observed with VLBA at 5 GHz as part of a polarization survey of BL-Lac objects Bondi2004 as a possible BL-Lac candidate. On the parsec scale (beam size of 3.2 1.7 mas) the source reveals a radio core with an extended jet towards the south. The jet extends for about 10 milliarcseconds (mas) ( 15 pc at z=-0.0825 ).

Among the BL-Lac candidates, this source is the weakest object, and has the steepest radio spectrum. It is also the only object not showing polarized emission neither in the jet or the core. In this galaxy, the narrow \ line is detected at blueshifted velocities with respect to the systemic.

sources.summary: The paper gives J0809+3455 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0809+3455. In the FIRST image, this source (also known as B2 0806+35) reveals a two-sided large-scale emission, and a nuclear component with a bright one-sided jet to the south. The NVSS image shows a northern extension (more than 4 in size) confirming the asymmetric brightness of the kiloparsec-scale source. In our VLBA image at 8 GHz, we detect a one-sided structure in agreement with the FIRST bright jet and the VLBA map at 5 GHz by Bondi et al. (2004).

sources.summary: MAGIC upper limits constrain the modeled BZB J0809+3455 spectrum, and the model classifies it as non-EHBL with a synchrotron peak below 10^17 Hz.

quotes.quote: The MAGIC upper limits between 200 and 700 GeV constrain well the modeled spectrum for BZB J0809+3455 (Fig. fig:SEDs_undetection (b)).

Three different mechanisms were considered in the samples to form the synchrotron peak: the intrinsic break in the electron spectrum (1ES 0229+200, 1ES 0927+500, BZB J0809+3455, RGB J2313+147), the maximum electron energy (RBS 0723 and RGB J2042+244), and the cooling break (1ES 1426+428 and 1ES 2037+521).

According to the model, 1ES 0229+200 remains the source of the sample with the most extreme synchrotron peak, while RGB J2313+147 and BZB J0809+3455 are non-EHBL sources, having their peak below the defined threshold of 10 ^ 17 Hz.

gmw_00001489.name: J0809+5218
gmw_00001489.overview: J0809+5218 is a high-energy peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1105.3345, arXiv:1110.0040] that hosts a two-sided parsec-scale jet [arXiv:1105.3345] and a collimated radio jet [arXiv:1309.2774]. The blazar exhibits very-high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:0901.1235, arXiv:1110.0040] and displays long-term optical and radio variability, including a major outburst [arXiv:1606.06431].

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 0806+524 as a TeV BL Lac whose VERITAS emission may be produced by inverse Compton scattering from centrifugally accelerated electrons.

quotes.quote: Let us consider the typical parameters of 1ES 0806+524: the black hole mass, M_ BH 5 10^8M_ , ( M_ is the solar mass) and the bolometric luminosity, L 7 10^ 44 erg/s .

According to the observations of VERITAS, performed from November 2006 to April 2008, the blazar 1ES0806+524, has the VHE emission with the intrinsic photon spectrum, _ i = (2.8 0.5) , and the intrinsic integral flux, F _ _ >0.3TeV = (4.4 1.1) 10^ 12 cm^ 2 s^ 1 .

For 1ES 0806+524 no infrared data are published so far, on the other hand, since the TeV emission is detected, therefore the infrared luminosity of 1ES 0806+524 must be less than 5.6 10^ 7 L_ Edd 3.6 10^ 40 erg/s (see Eq. ( tau )). One has to note that the centrifugal acceleration leads to the TeV variability timescale of the order (1-2)days . It is worth noting that TeV blazars exhibit the variability on hour to minute timescales, but this particular feature is not detected for 1ES 0806+524. As our investigation shows, the centrifugally accelerated outflows may provide the detected VHE emission of 1ES 0806+524 via the ICS if the parameters are chosen appropriately.

sources.summary: The paper reports first intranight optical monitoring of J0809 + 5218, noting a two-sided parsec-scale jet, no detected INOV, and longer-term fading and brightening.

quotes.quote: J0809 + 5218 (1ES 0806 + 524; z =0.138 ) To our knowledge, the present work is the first intranight optical monitoring of this HBL (4 nights, Table 3). A highly unusual aspect of this source, as revealed by the 5 GHz VLBI observations, is a two-sided jet structure on parsec scale straddling the core (Chen et al. \ 2006), which itself has a brightness temperature of 10^ 10 K, markedly lower than the inverse-Compton limit (Kellermann Paulini-Toth 1969). These unusual characteristics are, however, consistent with the absence of strong relativistic boosting in this HBL, as also reflected by its quite stable GeV flux (Chen et al. \ 2006). On none of the 4 nights did we detect INOV in this blazar (Table 3), although on longer term ( 10 month timescale), a clear fading by 0.45 mag was detected. Thereafter, a distinct brightening by 0.04 mag occured between the observations taken 4 days apart (Fig. \ 3).

sources.summary: VERITAS observed 1ES 0806+524, detected a point-like VHE excess with a soft 300-700 GeV spectrum, found no variability, and modeled its contemporaneous SED with one-zone SSC emission.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0806+524 is an HBL at a redshift of z = 0.138 . It was recommended as a likely VHE emitter by , and was observed by VERITAS for 65 h of quality-selected live time in 2006-08, largely during the commissioning of the instrument . A point-like excess (VER J0809+523) of 245 VHE rays is detected, corresponding to a statistical significance of 6.3 . The VHE spectrum observed between 300 GeV and 700 GeV is soft, with photon index = 3.6 1.0 , and the measured flux above 300 GeV is 1.8 Crab. No VHE flux variability is observed within limited statistics. An SED was generated using contemporaneous MWL observations and is reasonably described by a one-zone synchrotron-self-Compton (SSC) model.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 0806+524 as an HBL with VERITAS VHE detection, a power-law photon spectrum, Swift/Fermi data, and no significant month-scale variability.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0806+524. A HBL object was detected by VERITAS in VHE gamma-ray regime between November 2006 and April 2008, and no significant variability on months timescale was found (Acciari et al. 2009c). The observed photon spectrum from November 2007 to April 2008 can be fitted by a power law with 3.6 between 300 to 700 GeV. The integral flux above 300 GeV is 2.2 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , corresponding to 1.8 of Crab flux. The data obtained quasi-simultaneously by Swift and VERITAS observations (Acciari et al. 2009c) and the spectrum butterfly of Fermi /LAT observations (Abdo et al. 2009) are considered in this work.

sources.summary: The paper gives J0809+5218 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0809+5218. This BL Lac shows a point-like structure in NVSS (total flux density 183.8 mJy) and FIRST images, as well with the VLA in A configuration (Giroletti et al. 2004). Our VLBA maps display, at both frequencies, a collimated jet oriented at NE, but moving after a few mas in N direction, with a large PA change, moving to the same PA visible in 5 GHz VLBA images (Giroletti et al 2004b; MOJAVE Survey, Lister et al. 2009a, 2009b), and VLA images (Helmboldt et al.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 0806+524 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0806+524 : This source is close to the median flux density of the sample in both wavebands. Both light curves show very little variability before MJD 55400, after which there is a clear outburst in both wavebands. The peak in the optical is reached 200 days before the peak in radio, but due to a gap in the optical light curve we cannot exclude the possibility of a second peak at the time of the radio maxima. After the outburst the optical flux density steadily decreases and no significant trend is found in our trend analysis.

sources.summary: This known TeV BL Lac shows no significant flux variability in the paper's one-night monitoring and is described as quiescent in both gamma rays and long-term optical data.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0809.8+5218: This source is a known TeV BL Lac object 2009ApJ...690L.126A and its long term optical monitoring has been studied by 2014AJ....148..110M . They did not find any significant INOV during the period spanned over 6 years. Our one night of the optical monitoring has not revealed any significant flux variability and we find this object to be in a very low ray activity state during the epoch of the optical observation. The long term optical behavior of this object, as monitored by KAIT, also indicates the source to be in quiescence.

sources.summary: The paper gives 1ES 0806+524 an individual blazar variability note, summarizing TeV-band detections, GeV or hard-X-ray behavior, and variability classification.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0806+524 : This object was detected in the TeV band on several occasions at a flux level consistent with its 15 years averaged value. The analysis has revealed it to be non-variable and the Bayesian block analysis has also not found any significant variable flux states. The GeV band light curve highlighted a flaring activity in the time period MJD 55582 55852 in which the TeV band flux shows an enhancement as well. The source was also detected with MAGIC telescopes during this flaring episode Figure fig:1 ; 2015MNRAS.451..739A .

gmw_00001490.name: J0810+4911
gmw_00001490.overview: J0810+4911 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774] classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1309.2774]. In NVSS and FIRST radio images, the source appears symmetrically extended at a position angle of 135 degrees [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0810+4911 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0810+4911. In NVSS and FIRST images this source appears symmetrically extended in PA 135 deg, with a flux density 10 mJy. The source is undetected above 0.6 mJy beam ^ 1 and 1.8 mJy beam ^ 1 at 8 and 15 GHz respectively. SC is 0.06. No counterpart in the 2LAC is found.

gmw_00001491.name: J0847+1133
gmw_00001491.overview: J0847+1133 is a BL Lacertae blazar [arXiv:1809.01173, arXiv:1603.02410] located at a redshift of z=0.198 [arXiv:1603.02410]. The source is unresolved on kiloparsec scales [arXiv:1309.2774], exhibits compact parsec-scale radio emission [arXiv:1809.01173], and displays both optical and very-high-energy gamma-ray variability [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper says J0847 + 1133 is LAT-detected but initially undetected at parsec scale likely due to VLBI observing problems; other EVN/VLBA data show compact emission and intermediate radio classification.

quotes.quote: The only LAT-detected sources undetected at parsec scale are J0847 + 1133 and J1534 + 3715. However, the lack of detection of J0847 + 1133 is likely due to observational problems during VLBI observations reported in Liuzzo2013 . Nevertheless, a compact emission has been observed with the European VLBI Network (EVN) at 5 GHz mantovani15 and with VLBA at 8 GHz bourda10 for this source.

Among the 23 LAT-detected sources, 20 are classified as DD, and 3 as LD (J0847 + 1133, J1341 + 3959, and J1534 + 3715). All three newly detected ray sources are classified as DD. European VLBI Network and VLBA observations at 5 GHz and 8 GHz, respectively, and a CD of 5.1 suggests an intermediate classification of J0847 + 1133 between LD and DD.

sources.summary: The paper gives J0847+1133 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0847+1133. This source is unresolved on kiloparsec-scale with flux density 33 mJy. In our VLBA images, due to observational problems, it is undetected at both frequencies, with an upper limit of 15.3 mJy beam ^ 1 at 8 GHz and 3 mJy beam ^ 1 at 15 GHz. Bourda et al. (2010) revealed a VLBI flux density of 6 mJy in X band.

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB J0847+115 as an HBL blazar announced as a VHE source, with extreme-HBL characteristics, reported variability, and a marginally consistent VERITAS upper limit.

quotes.quote: RGB J0847+115 (HBL, z=0.198 ) VHE emission from this blazar was announced by \ in 2014, at a flux corresponding to 2.5 Crab above 200 GeV Magic0847 . No spectral information is currently available, but the \ collaboration reported a preliminary classification of the source as an extreme-HBL, with synchrotron peak-frequency in hard-X-rays, and inverse-Compton peak-frequency at TeV energies. Evidence of VHE and optical variability was also claimed by . The \ upper limit ( 2.0 of the Crab Nebula flux above 180 GeV) is marginally consistent with the preliminary flux estimate by , and could be related to variability of the VHE emission.

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB 0847+115 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: RGB 0847+115 : Among the weakest sources in our sample in both optical and radio. The calculation of the radio modulation index fails. The linear regression does not find significant trend in the radio light curve, but the polynomial fit does favour second order polynomial with decreasing trend. As such trend is clearly present in optical light curve, subtracting the ``polynomial'' (which in this case is just a line) decreases the rms of the optical light curve significantly.

sources.summary: The paper notes RBS 0723 as a LAT-detected and VHE-detected extreme BL Lac candidate with a hard 2FGL photon index.

quotes.quote: In this region there are two sources already detected by LAT, RBS 0723 and 1ES 1426+428.
More recently RBS 0723 has been discovered at VHE too, by MAGIC mirzoyan14 .
This assumption is supported by the detection of 1ES 0927+500 and RBS 1510 only at high energy and by the hard photon index of RBS 0723 reported in the 2FGL, =1.48 0.16 .

sources.summary: MAGIC detects RBS 0723, but its soft, uncertain spectrum and large distance make it the lone detected source only marginally consistent with hard-TeV behavior.

quotes.quote: The analysis revealed firm VHE gamma-ray detection of three new sources, namely TXS 0210+515, RBS 0723, and 1ES 2037+521, and a hint of signal from RGB J2042+244.
The observed spectra are quite soft, with a spectral index softer than 2, and in the case of RBS 0723 reaching the value 3.60 0.79 , where the error is statistical only.
This suggests a hard-TeV nature for all detected sources but RBS 0723 whose spectrum is anyhow affected by large error bars and yet in agreement with the hard-TeV nature hypothesis.

gmw_00001492.name: J0850+3455
gmw_00001492.overview: J0850+3455 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774] that is unresolved at kiloparsec scales in NVSS images [arXiv:1309.2774]. VLBA data reveals a resolved morphology at both 8 and 15 GHz, with no associated gamma-ray emission detected [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0850+3455 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0850+3455. At kiloparsec-scales, this source is unresolved with a total flux density of 34.5 mJy in NVSS images. In our VLBA data, it shows a resolved morphology at both frequencies with total flux density of 28.6 and 27.1 mJy at 8 and 15 GHz respectively, in agreement with measurements of Bourda et al. (2010). No gamma-ray emission is found associated to this object.

gmw_00001493.name: J0903+4055
gmw_00001493.overview: J0903+4055 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774]. It exhibits an unresolved, point-like structure on kiloparsec scales and has no associated gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0903+4055 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0903+4055. This source is unresolved on kiloparsec-scales with a flux density of 35.8 mJy in the NVSS image. In our VLBA data, it shows a point-like structure with a flux density of 9.1 mJy at 8 GHz and 6.3 mJy at 15 GHz. No gamma-ray emission is associated to this object. We classify it as DD.

gmw_00001494.name: J0916+5238
gmw_00001494.overview: J0916+5238 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774] that has been reclassified as a BZB blazar [arXiv:2103.10861]. On kiloparsec scales, the source exhibits a core-jet structure [arXiv:1309.2774], while its optical spectrum is featureless [arXiv:2103.10861].

sources.summary: The paper gives J0916+5238 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0916+5238. On kiloparsec-scales, this BL Lac shows a core-jet structure with total flux density 140 mJy in NVSS data and some evidences of extended emission. In our 8 GHz VLBA maps, it has a one-sided morphology: the jet is elongated to the north with a flux density of 5.4 mJy while the central unresolved component shows a 24.1 mJy flux density. At 15 GHz, only the core is revealed with a flux density of 16.8 mJy. No gamma-ray counterpart is found in the 2 LAC for this source.

sources.summary: The paper reports 5BZG J0916+5238 as a BZG reclassified from LAMOST data as a BZB, with a featureless spectrum preventing confirmation of its previous redshift.

quotes.quote: These five transitional (i.e., changing look) sources were found in addition to 12 BZGs for which our analysis classifies them as BZBs, one with a different redshift estimate, 5BZG J0916+5238 having a LAMOST featureless spectrum that did not allow us to confirm its previous z= 0.190 estimate.
5BZG J0916+5238 is a BZG in the Roma-BzCAT, based on the spectrum of nass96 . We re-classified it as a BZB according to our LAMOST investigation.

gmw_00001495.name: J0930+4950
gmw_00001495.overview: J0930+4950 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1501.01974, arXiv:1205.4939]. It features a compact radio core [arXiv:1205.4939, arXiv:1309.2774] with weak, diffuse emission extending to the southeast [arXiv:1205.4939].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes two Swift epochs for 1ES 0927+500, finds consistent XRT fits, and reports a marginal full-band Fermi/LAT detection.

quotes.quote: For most of the other sources only one observation was available, except for RBS 1029 which was observed in November and December 2007, 1ES0927+500, observed in September 2010 and March 2011, and RBS 1510 in June, September, October 2011 and in January 2012.
In the two cases of 1ES 0927+500 and RBS 1029 we separately consider two spectra. The best fit parameters are perfectly consistent.
For RBS 1510 (TS=23.0) and 1ES0927+500 (TS=24.5) we had marginal detections while we could confirm RBS 0723, already present in both the 1FGL and 2FGL catalogs.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1ES 0927+500 has a compact radio core, weak diffuse southeastern emission, and measured EVN/MERLIN core parameters.

quotes.quote: This source was observed with VLA config A at 1.4 GHz, with a peak flux density 19.9 mJy and no information is available on the pc-scale morphology gir04a . Our EVN, MERLIN and EVN+MERLIN images (Fig. J ) shows that this source has a compact core, and the weak diffuse emission at south-east to the core is detected at EVN and EVN+MERLIN images. The measured core parameters are shown in Table table_model .

sources.summary: The paper gives J0930+4950 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J0930+4950. This source appears clearly point-like both at arcsecond scale with a NVSS total flux density of 21.3 mJy and in VLBA images (7.6 mJy at 8 GHz). No gamma-ray emission is found associated to this target. We classify it as DD.

gmw_00001496.name: J1012+3932
gmw_00001496.overview: J1012+3932 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774]. On kiloparsec scales, the source exhibits a point-like radio structure with a total flux density of 20 mJy [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J1012+3932 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1012+3932. On kiloparsec-scales, this object is point-like with NVSS total flux density of 20 mJy. In our VLBA images, it is not detected above 0.9 mJy beam ^ 1 and 1.5 mJy beam ^ 1 at 8 and 15 GHz, respectively. SC is 0.05. No gamma-ray counterpart is found in the 2LAC.

gmw_00001497.name: J1022+5124
gmw_00001497.overview: J1022+5124 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774]. On kiloparsec scales, it exhibits a point-like radio structure with a low flux density, but it is not detected on parsec scales and lacks associated gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J1022+5124 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1022+5124. This target shows a kiloparsec-scale point-like structure with a low flux density in the NVSS image of 5.6 mJy. On parsec scale, it is not detected above 0.6 mJy beam ^ 1 and 1.8 mJy beam ^ 1 at 8 and 15 GHz respectively. No gamma-ray emission is found associated to this BL Lac. We classify it as U.

gmw_00001498.name: J1053+4929
gmw_00001498.overview: J1053+4929 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774] that appears unresolved on kiloparsec scales [arXiv:1309.2774]. Very Long Baseline Array observations show that the source is point-like at 15 GHz and exhibits a one-sided morphology at 8 GHz [arXiv:1309.2774].

sources.summary: The paper gives J1053+4929 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1053+4929. This BL Lac is unresolved on kiloparsec-scales with a total flux density of 65.5 mJy in NVSS data. In our VLBA maps, it is one-sided at 8 GHz and point-like at 15 GHz. Its _ core is 0.23 and it has an SC of 0.51. No counterpart in the 2LAC catalog is found.

gmw_00001499.name: J1058+5628
gmw_00001499.overview: J1058+5628 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1004.0239, arXiv:1309.2774] located at a redshift of approximately 0.143 [arXiv:1004.0239]. It exhibits a one-sided radio morphology at milliarcsecond scales [arXiv:1309.2774], a long-term decreasing optical trend [arXiv:1004.0239], and variability in gamma rays [arXiv:1307.8113].

sources.summary: The paper classifies BZB J1058+5628 as a BL Lac, separates host-galaxy contribution, and finds a long decreasing optical trend with broad quasi-periodic oscillations.

quotes.quote: BZBJ1058+5628 The first optical spectrum of this source was published by Marcha et al. (1996) giving a redshift z=0.41 form a strong [ O III ] emission line. Subsequent spectroscopic observations did not show any more these lines, but only the spectrum of the host galaxy with absorption lines at z=0.144 (Laurent-Muehleisen et al. 1988, Bade et al. 1988). A more recent spectrum can be found in the SDSS (Adelman-McCarthy et al. 2008), which provides the same redshift value (z=0.143) from several absorption lines of the host galaxy and the faint H + N [ II ] emission lines. The equivalent width of this blend is about 5 , giving a classification of BL Lac to this source.

The Asiago light curve covers about 11000 days and shows a definite monotonic decreasing trend of 2 magnitudes (0.053 mag/year). Superposed on this trend there are wide oscillations with peak-to-peak amplitude of about 1 mag, with a time scale of about 2500 days (7 years).

sources.summary: The paper treats BZB J1058+5628 as a TeV BL Lac candidate also chosen in earlier work, observed by VERITAS, and variable in gamma rays during those observations.

quotes.quote: Three of our sources appear as TeV candidates in the Tavecchio et al. (2010) selection: BZB J0109+1816, BZB J0136+3905 and BZB J1058+5628; the last source also in Costamante Ghisellini (2002). We note that VERITAS observations have been performed for 3 of our TBCs selected within the : BZB J0136+3905, BZB J1058+5628 and BZB J1243+3627 aliu12 .

On the other hand, BZB J1058+5628 was found variable in the rays by \ during the same period of the VERITAS observations. Thus, the non-detection at TeV energies of these three sources does not affect our selection that can only be verified with additional TeV observations.

sources.summary: The paper gives J1058+5628 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1058+5628. This BL Lac object has been studied by Nesci et al. (2010) in the B band: it is a quasi-periodic BL Lac, with an approximate period of 2590 days. In the radio band, it shows a kiloparsec unresolved structure with a total flux density in NVSS image of 230 mJy. At mas scale, this object has a one-sided morphology at both frequencies to the western direction with 8 GHz core flux density of 86.7 mJy, _ core 0.03, 8 GHz total flux density of 158.6 mJy and _ tot 0.33.

sources.summary: The paper treats TXS 1055 + 567 as a low-energy peaked BL Lac and plausible ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray source under one charge assignment, while noting the fit quality is limited.

quotes.quote: TXS 1055 + 567. This is an outstanding low-energy peaked BL Lac type object listed as a confirmed ``BL'' in the V eron catalog . With its visual magnitude of 15^ m .8 , strong dominance of the optical over X-ray emission and possible association with a EGRET sub-GeV source, this object satisfies all conditions for a BL Lac to be a potential cosmic-ray source, as discussed in Refs. . There is a recent evidence, based on the high-precision HiRes stereo data, that the objects of this particular subclass of BL Lac's may emit neutral ultra-high-energy particles . This makes its association with the cluster at the charge assignment N1 --- with a neutral Yakutsk primary --- rather plausible, despite a comparatively low quality of the fit ( ^2/ d.o.f. =4.72/8 ; the BL Lac position corresponds almost exactly to the highest-probability point).

gmw_00001500.name: J1120+4212
gmw_00001500.overview: J1120+4212 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1701.04305, arXiv:1309.2774] that is detected as a point-like radio source in X-ray surveys [arXiv:1701.04305]. Very Long Baseline Array observations reveal a one-sided radio jet structure [arXiv:1309.2774], while its optical spectrum is featureless [arXiv:1701.04305].

sources.summary: The paper treats BZB J1120+4212/RBS 0970 as a BL Lac with uncertain earlier absorption features, finding a featureless GTC spectrum and a redshift lower limit.

quotes.quote: BZB J1120+4212 : This object (also known as RBS 0970) is a point-like radio source detected by various X-ray surveys see e.g. giommi2005 . Optical spectral classification of the source as BLL was proposed by perlman1996 on the basis of the quasi-featureless spectrum. They claim the detection of starlight absorption features at z = 0.124. However, based on the spectrum reproduced in their Fig. 4, the reliability of this features is quite uncertain. This redshift is not confirmed in other spectra obtained by white2000 and massaro2014 .
Also the spectrum obtained by SDSS (J112048.06+421212.4) appears to us featureless. Our spectrum with SNR 100-190 is featureless and we set a lower limit of z > 0.28.

sources.summary: The paper gives J1120+4212 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1120+4212. This source has a point-like morphology on arcsecond scale with a NVSS total flux density of 23.5 mJy. Our VLBA maps revealed a one-sided structure at both frequencies, with a total 8 GHz flux density of 14.8 mJy and 8 GHz jet flux density of 4.7 mJy. The _ tot is 0.54, while SC is 0.83. Moreover, it is a LAT BL Lac (Table tab_multi ).

gmw_00001501.name: J1136+6737
gmw_00001501.overview: J1136+6737 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1309.2774] classified as a high-frequency peaked BL Lac object at a redshift of z=0.134 [arXiv:1603.02410]. It exhibits a one-sided radio jet morphology at 15 GHz [arXiv:1309.2774] and is associated with gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1309.2774, arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper gives J1136+6737 an individual BL Lac radio-structure entry, describing its arcsecond or VLBA morphology, flux measurements, gamma-ray association, and source classification.

quotes.quote: J1136+6737. This BL Lac has a kiloparsec-scale point-like structure, and is also point-like in our 8 GHz VLBA data with an SC 0.55. At 15 GHz, it is resolved into a one-sided jet morphology with _ core 0.29, _ tot 0.25, and NE jet flux density of 9.6 mJy. Gamma-ray emission is found associated with this object (Table tab_multi ). We classify it as DD.

sources.summary: The paper records RX J1136.5+6737 as an HBL with a recent VHE detection; the VERITAS upper limit above 290 GeV is consistent with that preliminary flux.

quotes.quote: RX J1136.5+6737 (HBL, z=0.134 ) The \ collaboration recently reported the detection of this source at a flux of 1.5 Crab above 200 GeV, in 20 hours of observations between January and April 2014 1136Magic . No spectral information is available at the present time. The \ upper limit ( 5.2 Crab above 290 GeV) is fully consistent with the preliminary flux estimate by .

gmw_00001502.name: J1145-0340
