sources.summary: The paper places 0548 322 in a giant elliptical galaxy, cites previous 8 and 15 GHz core-jet observations, and reports only a faint 2.3 GHz core.

quotes.quote: 0548 322: The source resides in a giant elliptical galaxy in the center of Abell S0549 falomo95 . 0548 322 has also been observed by piner13 and piner14 at 8 and 15 GHz. These observations reveal an unresolved core and a faint jet pointing towards the northeast with an opening angle of 13.4 . Our 2.3 GHz observations, on the other hand, due to the low image sensitivity show only the core with a flux density of 30 mJy.

gmw_00001612.name: J0839+1112
gmw_00001612.overview: J0839+1112 is a star-forming galaxy at a redshift of z=0.7866 [arXiv:1502.07895]. It has a star formation rate of 3.4 solar masses per year and hosts a modeled galactic wind outflow with a speed of 105 kilometers per second [arXiv:1502.07895].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0839+1112 as the largest-impact-parameter wind-pair, reports its emission properties, morphology, star formation rate, redshift, and modeled outflow speed.

quotes.quote: In our sample, the galaxy towards J0839+1112 has the largest impact parameter of b of 26.8 kpc. With an \ flux of 1.53 10^ 16 , an inclination i of 72 ^ and an azimuthal angle of 59 ^ , this galaxy also belongs to the wind-pair subsample defined in section section:azimuthal . Its SFR is 3.4 \ , and it has a redshift of z= 0.7866. In Figure fig:flux_2 we compare archival HST/WFPC2 (F702W filter) images to our SINFONI \ data.

Figure fig:J0839 shows the simulated profile and the \ absorption from the UVES data (right column of the figure). We constrained an outflow speed V_ out of 105 10 and a cone opening angle of _ max of 30 5 ^ . The impact parameter b is too high to consider any contribution from the galaxy.

gmw_00001613.name: LEDA 55267
gmw_00001613.overview: LEDA 55267 is a Fanaroff-Riley class 0 radio galaxy that exhibits gamma-ray emission above 1 GeV [arXiv:2108.11701]. This emission is likely to originate from misaligned jets [arXiv:2108.11701].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 55267 as one of three FR0 radio galaxies with significant LAT gamma-ray emission, a FIRST radio association, and Swift luminosity ratios consistent with misaligned jets.

quotes.quote: The LAT data analysis of 108 FR0 radio galaxies has revealed a significant ray emission from 3 objects above 1 GeV. The residual TS maps of these sources are shown in Figure  fig:ts_map along with their radio and optimized ray positions. The derived spectral parameters are provided in Table  tab:basic . To ascertain the association of the ray source with the FR0s, 1.4 GHz flux density maps from the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters (FIRST) survey were inspected. Two out of three FR0 galaxies, LEDA 55267 and LEDA 57137, were found to be the brightest radio sources within the 95 uncertainty region associated with the optimized ray position.

Using the Swift X-ray telescope observations, R_ /X was determined as 1.7, 1.9, and 1.4 for LEDA 55267, LEDA 57137, and LEDA 58287, respectively, similar to other FR0 galaxies. Altogether, the ray emission in FR0 systems is likely to be originated from misaligned jets.

gmw_00001614.name: LEDA 57137
gmw_00001614.overview: LEDA 57137 is a Fanaroff-Riley class 0 radio galaxy that exhibits gamma-ray emission above 1 GeV [arXiv:2108.11701]. This emission is likely to originate from misaligned jets [arXiv:2108.11701].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 57137 as one of three FR0 radio galaxies with significant LAT gamma-ray emission, a FIRST radio association, and Swift luminosity ratios consistent with misaligned jets.

quotes.quote: The LAT data analysis of 108 FR0 radio galaxies has revealed a significant ray emission from 3 objects above 1 GeV. The residual TS maps of these sources are shown in Figure  fig:ts_map along with their radio and optimized ray positions. The derived spectral parameters are provided in Table  tab:basic . To ascertain the association of the ray source with the FR0s, 1.4 GHz flux density maps from the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters (FIRST) survey were inspected. Two out of three FR0 galaxies, LEDA 55267 and LEDA 57137, were found to be the brightest radio sources within the 95 uncertainty region associated with the optimized ray position.

Using the Swift X-ray telescope observations, R_ /X was determined as 1.7, 1.9, and 1.4 for LEDA 55267, LEDA 57137, and LEDA 58287, respectively, similar to other FR0 galaxies. Altogether, the ray emission in FR0 systems is likely to be originated from misaligned jets.

gmw_00001615.name: HATLAS J090426.9+015448
gmw_00001615.overview: HATLAS J090426.9+015448 is a faint, high-redshift narrow-line radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.37 [arXiv:1610.07699]. It contains a large reservoir of molecular gas and dust-obscured star formation, with double-peaked CO emission that may indicate a rotating disk or an ongoing merger [arXiv:1610.07699].

sources.summary: The paper presents HATLAS J090426.9+015448 as a faint high-redshift narrow-line radio galaxy with abundant molecular gas, dust-obscured star formation, broad double-peaked CO emission, and possible disk or merger kinematics.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The fitted position for HATLAS J090426.9+015448 is at right ascension 09h04m26.711s 0.068 s and declination +01 54 49.67 0.58

quotes.quote: Observations using the 7 mm receiver system on the Australia Telescope Compact Array have revealed large reservoirs of molecular gas in two high-redshift radio galaxies: HATLAS J090426.9+015448 ( z = 2.37) and HATLAS J140930.4+003803 ( z = 2.04). Optically the targets are very faint, and spectroscopy classifies them as narrow-line radio galaxies. In addition to harbouring an active galactic nucleus the targets share many characteristics of sub-mm galaxies. Far-infrared data from Herschel ATLAS suggest high levels of dust ( > 10 ^ 9 M _ ) and a correspondingly large amount of obscured star formation ( 1000 M _ / yr).

The molecular gas is traced via the J = 1 0 transition of ^ 12 CO, its luminosity implying total H _ 2 masses of (1.7 0.3) 10 ^ 11 and (9.5 2.4) 10 ^ 10 ( _ CO /0.8) M _ in HATLAS J090426.9+015448 and HATLAS J140930.4+003803 respectively. Both galaxies exhibit molecular line emission over a broad ( 1000 km/s) velocity range, and feature double-peaked profiles. We interpret this as evidence of either a large rotating disk or an on-going merger. Gas depletion timescales are 100 Myr.

gmw_00001616.name: HATLAS J140930.4+003803
gmw_00001616.overview: HATLAS J140930.4+003803 is a narrow-line radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.04 [arXiv:1610.07699]. It contains massive reservoirs of dust and molecular gas that support intense, obscured star formation, with double-peaked CO emission that may indicate an ongoing merger or a large rotating disk [arXiv:1610.07699].

sources.summary: The paper describes HATLAS J140930.4+003803 as a z = 2.04 narrow-line radio galaxy with massive molecular gas and dust reservoirs, intense obscured star formation, and broad double-peaked CO emission.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: For HATLAS J140930.4+003803 the fitted right ascension and declination are 14h09m30.211s 0.020 s and +00 38 04.62 0.23

quotes.quote: Observations using the 7 mm receiver system on the Australia Telescope Compact Array have revealed large reservoirs of molecular gas in two high-redshift radio galaxies: HATLAS J090426.9+015448 ( z = 2.37) and HATLAS J140930.4+003803 ( z = 2.04). Optically the targets are very faint, and spectroscopy classifies them as narrow-line radio galaxies. In addition to harbouring an active galactic nucleus the targets share many characteristics of sub-mm galaxies. Far-infrared data from Herschel ATLAS suggest high levels of dust ( > 10 ^ 9 M _ ) and a correspondingly large amount of obscured star formation ( 1000 M _ / yr).

The molecular gas is traced via the J = 1 0 transition of ^ 12 CO, its luminosity implying total H _ 2 masses of (1.7 0.3) 10 ^ 11 and (9.5 2.4) 10 ^ 10 ( _ CO /0.8) M _ in HATLAS J090426.9+015448 and HATLAS J140930.4+003803 respectively. Both galaxies exhibit molecular line emission over a broad ( 1000 km/s) velocity range, and feature double-peaked profiles. We interpret this as evidence of either a large rotating disk or an on-going merger. Gas depletion timescales are 100 Myr.

gmw_00001617.name: TXS 1954+513
gmw_00001617.overview: TXS 1954+513 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1712.06314] and blazar [arXiv:1612.01139, arXiv:1804.03703] at a redshift of z = 1.2230 [arXiv:1612.01139]. It features a classical double-lobe radio structure [arXiv:1712.06314] and associated neutral hydrogen gas that is likely driven outward by its radio jet [arXiv:1612.01139].

sources.summary: The paper reports associated H i absorption toward the z = 1.2230 blazar TXS 1954+513, interpreting the blueshifted absorber as neutral gas likely driven outward by the radio jet.

quotes.quote: We have used the 610 MHz receivers of the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) to detect associated H i 21 cm absorption from the z = 1.2230 blazar TXS 1954+513. The GMRT H i 21 cm absorption is likely to arise against either the milli-arcsecond-scale core or the one-sided milli-arcsecond-scale radio jet, and is blueshifted by 328 km s ^ 1 from the blazar redshift. This is consistent with a scenario in which the H i cloud giving rise to the absorption is being driven outward by the radio jet. The integrated H i 21 cm optical depth is (0.716 0.037) km s ^ 1 , implying a high H i column density, N_ HI = (1.305 0.067) ( T_s/100 K ) 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 , for an assumed H i spin temperature of 100 K.

We use Nickel Telescope photometry of TXS 1954+513 to infer a high rest-frame 1216 \ luminosity of (4.1 1.2) 10^ 23 W Hz ^ 1 . The z = 1.2230 absorber towards TXS 1954+513 is only the fifth case of a detection of associated H i 21 cm absorption at z > 1 , and is also the first case of such a detection towards an active galactic nucleus (AGN) with a rest-frame ultraviolet luminosity 10^ 23 W Hz ^ 1 , demonstrating that neutral hydrogen can survive in AGN environments in the presence of high ultraviolet luminosities.

sources.summary: The paper reports RM in a knot of 1954+513, an extreme core RM, and an unreliable core-region gradient likely affected by spurious sign changes.

quotes.quote: Polarization observations of 6 radio--quasars (0133+476, 0420-014, 0745+241, 0906+430, 1633+382 and 1954+513) and 2 BL Lac objects (0256+075 and 1823+568) were carried out in a 24 hour session on November 2, 2008 using the VLBA.

In 1633+382 and 1954+513 we also find RM in the knots located at 4 and 3.5 mas from the core, respectively. Typical RM values are of the order of some krad/m ^2 , with 1633+382 and 1954+513 showing the most extreme values.

1954+513 shows indications of a gradient across the core region that seem to be faded out (or inverted) as we move less than 1 beam size away. This case is similar to one of the simulations of , where spurious rotation measures and sign changes occur, and thus, we do not consider this gradient reliable.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1954 + 513/OV 591 as a z=1.22 radio-loud quasar with a classical FR II double-lobe structure, trailing northern jet, and no counter-jet evidence.

quotes.quote: 1954 + 513 (J1955 + 5131) The source 1954 + 513 (OV 591) is a radio-loud quasar with a redshift of z = 1.22 and was detected with a fractional linear optical polarization below 3 1996ApJS..107..541L,2010A&A...518A..10V . Neither EGRET nor Fermi /LAT has detected this source. 1982AJ.....87..859P reported a classical core--double-lobe FR II structure oriented in the north--south direction with a total extent of 16 (130 kpc). The northern lobe has a trailing jet continuously connected with the core, in which a midway hotspot is seen at about 5 from the core. The naturally weighted 5-GHz VLA image shows that the southern component is slightly elongated toward the core, but there is no evidence of a counter-jet 1990AJ....100.1057K .

sources.summary: The paper reports a strong 7.5-year gamma-ray signal from 5BZQ J1955+5131, attributing its 3FGL absence to post-2014 variability and Galactic-plane sensitivity limits.

quotes.quote: 5BZQ J1955+5131. This blazar shows a strong \ signal when integrating over 7.5 yrs with Pass 8 data, but from the light curve (Fig. bzq1955 ) we measured significant flaring activity only after 2014. A likelihood analysis integrating Pass 7 data from Aug. 2008 up to Aug. 2012 (hereafter we refer to this as the 3FGL setup) have shown TS ^ Pass 7 _ 4.0 yrs 5.0, and therefore this source is out of the 3FGL catalog because of its variability. In addition, this blazar is close to the galactic plane (b = 11.7 ^ ), where the low-energy diffuse background is more intense thus hindering detections of faint \ sources.

gmw_00001618.name: 6C**0935+4348
gmw_00001618.overview: The radio galaxy 6C**0935+4348 is a faint K-z outlier with an uncertain identification and redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0612268]. Its discrepancy between magnitude and redshift suggests that its stellar content is not consistent with a massive host galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612268].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6C**0935+4348 as a very faint K-z outlier with uncertain identification/redshift, suggesting its stellar content may not match a massive host galaxy.

quotes.quote: The other is 6C**0935+4348 ( z_ spec = 2.321 , z_ est > 4.0 ), which is one of the faintest sources in the sample, with K > 21.7 mag in a 3-arcsec aperture. This source is two magnitudes fainter than the mean K-z relation, which makes it a substantial K-z outlier (see Fig. fig:kz_6cds ). However, one caveat should be added here: the identification and redshift for this source are uncertain (Paper I).

For both 6C**0935+4348 and 6C**1009+4327, the discrepancy between magnitude and redshift suggests that the stellar content is not consistent with that of a massive host-galaxy. One possibility is that these sources are not yet fully formed (e.g. Jarvis, van Breukelen Wilman 2005).

gmw_00001619.name: 6C**1009+4327
gmw_00001619.overview: The radio galaxy 6C**1009+4327 is a faint source with a spectroscopic redshift of 1.956 [arXiv:astro-ph/0612268]. Its spectrum shows weak Lyman-alpha emission, and its stellar content is inconsistent with that of a massive host galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612268].

sources.summary: The paper says 6C**1009+4327 has an overestimated photometric redshift, weak Lyman-alpha emission, and stellar content inconsistent with a massive host-galaxy.

quotes.quote: One is 6C**1009+4327 ( z_ spec = 1.956 , z_ est = 3.089 ), which is a very faint source ( K = 20.5 mag in a 3-arcsec aperture) with spectrum showing weak Lyman- emission.

For both 6C**0935+4348 and 6C**1009+4327, the discrepancy between magnitude and redshift suggests that the stellar content is not consistent with that of a massive host-galaxy. One possibility is that these sources are not yet fully formed (e.g. Jarvis, van Breukelen Wilman 2005). If, as a consequence of the `youth-redshift' degeneracy described by Blundell Rawlings (1999), we are preferentially observing young sources ( 10^ 7 yr), then in some cases this could be the first instance of accretion activity in those sources.

gmw_00001620.name: GRB 080905A host galaxy
gmw_00001620.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 080905A is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 0.122 [arXiv:1006.0487, arXiv:2112.04924]. It exhibits active star formation with several star-forming regions scattered across its structure [arXiv:1006.0487, arXiv:2112.04924].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 080905A host as a nearby, nearly face-on Sb/ spiral at z=0.1218, with bulge, disk, spiral arms, active star formation, an older burst site, and an 18.5 kpc burst offset.

quotes.quote: From the detected emission lines we measure the redshift of the GRB host galaxy to be z = 0.1218 0.0003 . Host morphology Visual inspection of the images in R and K bands shows the host to be a nearly face-on galaxy, with clear bulge, disk and spiral arm components. At least two spiral arms can be distinguished, one on either side of the galaxy, which are hard to see due to a great number of foreground stars.
The detection of spiral arms in combination with the detected emission and absorption lines allows us to loosely classify the host of GRB 080905A as an Sb/ galaxy.

The host galaxy is a spiral galaxy with active star formation, but GRB 080905A occured close to a spiral arm, dominated by a relatively old population, and on the opposite side of the galaxy from the spiral arm with most active star formation. Additionally, it was offset from the centre of the host galaxy by a projected radial distance of 18.5 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper studies the nearly face-on spiral host of GRB 080905A with MUSE/HST/ATCA, finding strong line emission, SFR about 1.6, and scattered star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: At a redshift of z =0.122, the nearly face-on spiral host of the short GRB 080905A is one of the closest short-GRB host galaxies identified so far. This made it a preferred target to explore spatially resolved star-formation and to investigate the afterglow position in the context of its star formation structures. We used VLT/MUSE integral-field unit observations, supplemented by ATCA 5.5/9.0 GHz radio-continuum measurements and publicly available HST data, to study the star-formation activity in the GRB 080905A host galaxy. The MUSE observations reveal that the entire host is characterized by strong line emission. Using the H line flux, we measure for the entire galaxy an SFR of about 1.6 , consistent with its non-detection by ATCA. Several individual star-forming regions are scattered across the host.

gmw_00001621.name: NGC 3239
gmw_00001621.overview: NGC 3239 is a nearby irregular galaxy located approximately 9.8 Mpc away [arXiv:1305.5789]. It is a gravitationally bound member of a galaxy group and exhibits a disturbed structure with patchy hydrogen distribution and possible tidal tails [arXiv:1407.3979].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3239 as the nearby irregular host of SN 2012A, adopts a 9.8 Mpc distance and low extinction, and uses pre-explosion Gemini imaging to search for the progenitor.

quotes.quote: We present the one-year long observing campaign of SN 2012A which exploded in the nearby (9.8 Mpc) irregular galaxy NGC 3239.

In the following we adopt a distance of 9.8 Mpc, i.e. a distance modulus of 29.96 mag with a formal error of 0.15 mag as reported by NED ( H_0=73 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ). We also derive the total extinction in the direction of NGC 3239 (Galactic plus host galaxy) as A_B=0.15 mag (see Section extinction ).

With this in mind, we searched for pre-explosion images of NGC 3239 in which we might detect and constrain the progenitor of SN 2012A. While there are no HST images of NGC 3239, the galaxy was observed prior to the explosion of SN 2012A with the Near InfraRed Imager and Spectrometer (NIRI) on the Gemini North Telescope Programme ID: GN-2006A-DD-2, PI: Michaud . NGC 3239 was observed on 2006 May 13 in K' with the f/14 camera on NIRI, which has a pixel scale of 0.05 arcsec pix ^ 1 across a 51 51arcsec ^ 2 field of view.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3239 as a disturbed group member with patchy H distribution, possible tidal tails, radio emission matching the optical disc, and weak polarized emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 3239. -- This galaxy is gravitationally bound with six other galaxies, forming a group (Huchra Geller ). The H distribution is patchy (Fig. f: a), showing a possible remnant of a disc structure and (probably tidal) tails. The present H II luminosity function is very similar to the LMC, including a supergiant H II region with the luminosity of 76 of 30 Dorarus (Krienke Hodge ).

The radio emission at 4.85 GHz corresponds well to the optical disc (Fig. f: b). Some very weak polarised emission at 3 level ( 0.24 Jy/b.a.) is also detected between the centre and the southern optical tail. At 8.35 GHz, the strongest radio emission is associated with the brightest H II regions (Fig. f: a).

gmw_00001622.name: NGC 4027
gmw_00001622.overview: NGC 4027 is a peculiar, asymmetric barred late-type spiral galaxy with a lopsided appearance caused by a massive spiral arm [arXiv:1205.1936, arXiv:1407.3979]. It is interacting with a smaller companion galaxy, NGC 4027A, which has resulted in an extended neutral hydrogen ring around the main galaxy [arXiv:1205.1936].

sources.summary: NGC 4027 is a lopsided one-armed galaxy interacting with NGC 4027A, with an HI ring, strong disk and nuclear radio emission, and concentrated southern-arm star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 4027 : This galaxy, like NGC 3445, appears to be a one armed spiral galaxy in the optical images; this is mainly due to the prominent northern spiral arm that gives it a lopsided appearance. The smaller southern spiral arm appears much more prominent in the HI images of the galaxy phookun.etal.1992 and extends down towards the smaller companion galaxy NGC 4027A. The net effect of the tidal interaction between NGC 4027 and NGC 4027A has resulted in an extended HI ring around NGC 4027. The star formation is distributed over the disk and and especially along the spiral arms of NGC 4027. The 1.4 GHz radio continuum flux for this galaxy is the highest in the sample (Table 2) and the emission is distributed over the disk and nuclear regions.

(iii) NGC 4027 : This galaxy shows the maximum radio emission in our sample (Figure 4). The net flux is 88 mJy and is mainly distributed around the southern spiral arm. The emission north of the galaxy center is patchy and does not follow the spiral pattern as closely as the southern arm. The emission along the southern spiral arm is concentrated at two locations.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4027 as a peculiar asymmetric barred late-type spiral whose radio emission covers the disc and whose western radio patch has uncertain association with the galaxy.

quotes.quote: NGC 4027. -- This is a peculiar, asymmetric late-type spiral with a small, separated companion (at RA _ 2000 = 11^h59^m29.4^s , Dec _ 2000 = 19 19 55 ). The galaxy has no bulge, and the nucleus is embedded in a bar (Eskridge et al. ). Its peculiar morphology and optical asymmetry is due to one of its spiral arms being bright and massive (Figs. f: ). The NGC 4027 resides in a small group of galaxies however, there is no unequivocal evidence of any encounter or merger effects.

With the low resolution of our maps at 4.85 and 8.35 GHz, the radio emission covers the whole galactic disc (Figs. f: and f: b, respectively). A patch of radio emission to the west, visible at both frequencies, has its counterpart in the NVSS map at 1.4 GHz. However, its physical relation with the galaxy is not clear.

gmw_00001623.name: NGC 4605
gmw_00001623.overview: NGC 4605 is a non-axisymmetric galaxy located at the edge of the M81/M82 group [arXiv:1407.3979, arXiv:1608.06264]. It features a peculiar morphology with strong disk emission [arXiv:1407.3979, arXiv:astro-ph/0701465], a system of fine emission filaments [arXiv:astro-ph/0701465], and a great amount of dust on its western side [arXiv:astro-ph/0701465].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4605 as an M81/M82-group edge object with peculiar morphology, noticeable disc radio emission, limited optical comparison, and no detected polarized emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 4605. -- The galaxy is located at the edge of M81/M82 group, and its rigid-body rotation creates problems in the CDM modelling of this object (Sofue ). Its peculiar morphology is similar to NGC 2976, which does not feature either a central component or a distinct spiral pattern. The radio emission from the galactic disc is quite noticeable at 4.85 GHz (Fig. f: d), especially in the map with the 20 Jy background source to the south subtracted (Fig. f:sub a). However, the resolution of our observations did not allow us to make a more detailed comparison with the optical data.

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 4605 had not previously been imaged in H, shows strong disk emission, fine filaments, a southern diffuse filament, and dust.

quotes.quote: NGC 4605 . It is surprising, but this bright galaxy has never been imaged before in the H line. A powerful emission is observed in its disk, which exceeds in flux the H emission from another Sdm galaxy, NGC 2976. A system of fine emission filaments attaching to the emission disk of NGC 4605 makes the galaxy looking like a bristled up sea-horse. A long diffuse filament extends on the southern side of the disk while the western side of the disk is likely to contain a great amount of dust.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4605 as non-axisymmetric, without strong bar evidence, and possibly poorly fit by MOND because of photometric and kinematic asymmetries.

quotes.quote: It is clear from the 3.6 m image that NGC 4605 is not axisymmetrical.
Indeed, hereafter S05 sim05 did not find any strong evidence for a bar in NGC 4605.

However, uncertainties in the ellipticity caused by the non-axisymmetric structures seen in both photometric and kinematic maps (see Figure fig:images1 and S05) might be the reason why MOND does not correctly predict the rotation curve of NGC 4605.

gmw_00001624.name: NGC 4618
gmw_00001624.overview: NGC 4618 is a lopsided, barred Magellanic spiral galaxy [arXiv:1206.4104, arXiv:2105.13394] that exhibits a single distorted spiral arm [arXiv:1407.3979, arXiv:1804.00469] and a surrounding neutral hydrogen ring [arXiv:1206.4104]. The galaxy shows signs of tidal disturbance [arXiv:1407.3979], though its apparent interaction with the nearby galaxy NGC 4625 is contested by discrepant distance measurements [arXiv:1804.00469].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4618 as tidally disturbed, with offset radio emission tied to its southern spiral arm and HI ring, plus ordered magnetic fields in the outer galaxy.

quotes.quote: NGC 4618. -- This galaxy manifests evidence of tidal interactions: there is one prominent spiral arm to the south and a bar extending further to the southwest (Fig. f: e). The peak of total radio emission at both 4.85 and 8.35 GHz (Fig. f: ) is set off from the brightest central region in the H map, towards the southern spiral arm. The H i distribution (Kaczmarek Wilcots ) is therefore compatible with radio emission extending from the bar to the south, which further joins a bright H i ring encircling the entire galaxy. The polarised radio emission at 4.85 GHz is detected in the SE part, where the B-vectors are oriented roughly along the southern optical spiral arm and resemble other tidally perturbed galaxies (Drzazga et al. ).

The degree of polarisation of about 3 at 4.85 GHz proves the presence of ordered magnetic fields in the outer part of the galaxy. At 8.35 GHz, a spot of polarised signal at about 3 .m.s. noise level is visible in the southwest part of the disc but not in the southeast part (Fig. f: ). It is possible that the noise distribution at 8.35 GHz is not uniform in this field: this frequency is more sensitive to weather conditions and scanning effects than at 4.85 GHz. Thus, we do not consider this spot as a proper detection of polarisation at 8.35 GHz.

sources.summary: NGC 4618 is described as a prototypical Magellanic spiral with a stellar bar, lopsided one-armed structure, bar-centered star formation, and an extended H I ring with central holes.

quotes.quote: From its morphology alone, NGC 4618 appears to be a prototypical Magellanic spiral, with an obvious stellar bar and a lopsided one-armed spiral structure. Both galaxies are host to on-going star formation; in NGC 4618 most of the H I regions lie near the central bar, while in NGC 4625 the star formation is nearly uniformly distributed across its disk. The goals of this work are to re-examine the issue of whether these two galaxies are indeed interacting and what effect such an interaction may be having on both NGC 4618 and NGC 4625, particularly with regard to star formation.

The channel maps reveal the complex extended structure of NGC 4618 which we characterize as a ring. This extended emission is notable as the gas north of the main body of NGC 4618 most readily seen in the channels displayed in the upper right panel of Figure 1. By the end of the cube in the lower left panel of Figure 1, the extended gas is seen largely to the south of the main body of NGC 4618. The large, extended H I ring surrounding the disk of NGC 4618, as well as a number of holes in the H I distribution in the central regions of the galaxy, are evident in the moment 0 map in Figures 2 and 3.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4618 as a peculiar distorted Sdm galaxy whose apparent interaction with NGC 4625 is challenged by discrepant TRGB distances.

quotes.quote: NGC 4618. This is a peculiar Sdm galaxy Arp 023 = VV073 with a distorted single-arm structure. Together with the nearby distorted Sdm galaxy NGC 4625 it forms a pair KPG349 with a projected separation of 9' and radial velocity difference of 70 . Surprisingly, the TRGB distance to NGC 4618, 7.66 0.22 Mpc, differs significantly from that of NGC 4625, 11.75 0.59 Mpc mcquinn2017 .

We re-measured the TRGB-distance to NGC 4625 using its archival HST images and obtained a distance of 12.61 0.49 Mpc in satisfactory agreement with the estimate by mcquinn2017 . This is an astonishing, instructive case where a small angular separation between two galaxies, their small radial velocity difference, as well as apparent signs of interaction, do not ensure the spatial proximity of the galaxies. We use in the distance measurement and show in the CMD only stars in the outer part of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4618 as an asymmetric barred Magellanic spiral paired with NGC 4625, showing tidal interaction, disk breaks, and a complicated evolutionary history.

quotes.quote: NGC 4618 NGC 4618 is an SBm galaxy that forms a physical pair with NGC 4625. bertola1967 likened its morphology to the LMC because of its strong bar and its main arm that encompasses most of the central region. NGC 4618 has a bar that is not centered on the nucleus eskridge2002 , and this offset can be seen in the photometric profiles in Figure fig:ngc4618_fulldata . The tidal interaction between NGC 4618 and NGC 4625 is seen in the morphology and HI distributions of both (Figure fig:ngc4618_4panel ).

The asymmetry of NGC 4618 makes the radial analysis difficult to interpret, but was a good candidate for the individual stellar complex analysis. staudaher2019 used the deep 3.6 m image of NGC 4618 to quantify the breaks in the stellar disc, finding a bulge and possible four disc components. A complicated evolutionary history, whether through stellar migration, tidal interactions, mergers, or some mixture of all these factors, may be an explanation for the changes in NGC 4618's stellar disc.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4618 as a lopsided galaxy whose SFR infrared faintness is mainly due to compact physical size rather than low surface brightness.

quotes.quote: The star-forming regions (SFRs) in the peculiar galaxies NGC 660, NGC 1512, NGC 4395 and NGC 4618 are studied. The relationships between such characteristics of star forming regions as UV, nearand far-IR fluxes, as well as in the H and HI lines, surface brightness in these ranges, and the scatter of the radial velocities of ionized and neutral hydrogen are considered. It is shown that in all the galaxies considered, the IR fluxes from SFRs are less than in the “normal” galaxies, but for different reasons: in the galaxies with signs of recent interaction NGC 660 and NGC 1512, this is due to the low surface brightness of SFRs; in the lopsided galaxies NGC 4395 and NGC 4618, the low brightness of SFRs in the infrared range is due to their compact size.

Thus, although the asymmetry of disk galaxies is a fairly common property ( ), in two specific cases (NGC 4395 and NGC 4618) we see noticeable differences from both interacting and “normal” galaxies. It should be noted that the galaxy NGC 4395 is isolated, and the galaxy NGC 4618 although usually considered in conjunction with the galaxy NGC 4625, may also not be interacting ( ). It is obvious that the interaction is not the only reason for the formation of SFRs with a small flux in the IR range.

gmw_00001625.name: UGC 11861
gmw_00001625.overview: UGC 11861 is an isolated irregular galaxy [arXiv:1504.05921, arXiv:1407.3979] that features a weak bar and a few dispersed spiral arms [arXiv:1407.3979]. Its radio emission roughly matches its optical extent [arXiv:1407.3979], and its rotation curves are best reproduced by cored dark matter halo models [arXiv:1504.05921].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes UGC 11861 as an irregular galaxy requiring dark matter beyond stars and gas, finding cored halo models best reproduce its H and HI dark-matter rotation curves.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2002 In this article we investigate the outer and inner mass distributions of the irregular galaxies UGC 4284 and UGC 11861, taking advantage of published HI and H high resolution rotation curves and constraining the stellar disk of both galaxies throughout stellar population synthesis studies. In addition we take into account the gas content of both galaxies deriving the HI + He rotation curve. The deduced baryonic rotation curves (star+gas) are inadequate to account for the total mass of UGC 4284 and UGC 11861, for that reason we examine the possibility of dark matter to explain the incongruity between the observed HI and H rotation curves of UGC 4284 and UGC 11861 and the derived baryonic rotation curves. We consider NFW, Burkert, DiCintio, Einasto, and the Stadel dark matter halos, to analyse the dark matter content of UGC 4284 and UGC 11861. The principal results of this work are that cored dark matter models better reproduce the dark matter H and HI rotation curves of UGC 11861 and the dark matter HI rotation curve of UGC 4284, while, the H rotation curve of UGC 4284 is better reproduced by a cuspy DiCintio DM model.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 11861 as the largest and most massive isolated galaxy in the sample, with weak bar/spiral structure and radio emission roughly matching its optical extent.

quotes.quote: UGC 11861. -- This isolated galaxy is the largest (with a diameter of 18.2 kpc) and the most massive ( M_ HI 9 10^9 M _ ) object in our sample. It shows a weak bar and a few dispersed spiral arms in optical images (Fig. f: f). The radio emission does not manifest any special features when observed with the resolution of 2.5 and corresponds roughly to the optical extent of the object.

gmw_00001626.name: UGC 628
gmw_00001626.overview: UGC 628 is a dark-matter-dominated, low surface brightness barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:0904.3442, arXiv:1507.03011] classified as a Magellanic spiral [arXiv:0904.3442, arXiv:1507.03011]. Its star formation is highly clustered and concentrated in the outer disk [arXiv:1507.03011], while its central bar region has been largely inactive for several billion years [arXiv:2001.08280].

sources.summary: The paper measures UGC 628 as a dark-matter-dominated low surface brightness barred spiral whose bar pattern speed is unusually low, making the bar slow.

quotes.quote: We report on an estimate of the bar pattern speed _p for the low surface brightness spiral galaxy UGC 628. We applied the Tremaine-Weinberg method to high resolution \ velocity and integrated emission maps of this dark matter dominated galaxy. The Tremaine-Weinberg method estimates a bar pattern speed of (11.3 2.0) \ for UGC 628, which is among the lowest values found for a spiral galaxy.
It implies that the bar in UGC 628 is slow.

Indeed, UGC 628 is a Magellanic barred, low surface brightness galaxy (LSB, hereafter). Like most galaxies of this kind, its mass is entirely dominated by dark matter at all galactocentric radii (de Blok Bosma 2002).

sources.summary: The paper studies UGC 628 as an LSB spiral, measuring its spatially resolved emission lines, clustered outer-disk star formation, gas metallicity, dark-matter dominance, and barred structure.

quotes.quote: Here we present the spatially-resolved optical spectra of UGC 628, observed with the VIRUS-P IFU at the 2.7-m Harlen J. \ Smith Telescope at the McDonald Observatory, and utilize emission-line diagnostics to determine the rate and distribution of star formation as well as the gas-phase metallicity and metallicity gradient. We find highly clustered star formation throughout UGC 628, excluding the core regions, and a log(O/H) metallicity around 4.2 , with more metal rich regions near the edges of the galactic disk. Based on the emission-line diagnostics alone, the current mode of star formation, slow and concentrated in the outer disk, appears to have dominated for quite some time, although there are clear signs of a much older stellar population formed in a more standard inside-out fashion.

UGC 628 is an extreme late-type spiral (see Figure fig:spitzerRGB (a)), classified as a Magellanic Spiral (Sm) by . With a redshift of z = 0.018166 Schneider1990 , the proper distance to UGC 628 is approximately 78Mpc. Like many LSB galaxies, UGC 628 is dark matter dominated, with nearly solid-body rotation out to 3 kpc deBlokBosma2002 , but, unlike most LSB disks, UGC 628 is a barred spiral.

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs UGC 628 as an LSB galaxy whose appearance changed from an earlier high-surface-brightness spiral to a recent starburst, with early bar formation and patchy recent star formation.

quotes.quote: Our main findings with respect to UGC 628 are: a) the visible properties of UGC 628 have varied over time, appearing as a high surface brightness spiral earlier than 8 Gyr ago and a starburst galaxy during a recent episode of star formation several tens of Myr ago, b) the central bar/core region was established early, around 8-10 Gyr ago, but has been largely inactive since, and ) star formation in the past 3 Gyr is best characterised as patchy and sporadic.

As mentioned above, the reconstructed star-formation history of UGC 628 is illustrated in timeline . Azimuthally averaged histories are shown in radial , and interesting galaxy-wide properties in each summarised in timeline . In order to compare to present-day galaxies, ubv shows a recreated UBV image of UGC 628 for each timestep, assuming present-day extinction. Note that because of this assumption, the data in ubv can only be used for gross characterization, and even then only with caution. Also note that ubv is not a recreation of the actual appearance of UGC 628 during these epochs since we are not correcting for the orbits of the stars, and many dynamical times have passed since the earliest timesteps.

gmw_00001627.name: ELAISP90 J164010+410502
gmw_00001627.overview: ELAISP90 J164010+410502 is a hyperluminous infrared galaxy and a broad-line, radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of 1.099 [arXiv:astro-ph/0107148]. It is detected in soft X-rays, shows no evidence of gravitational lensing, and may host a starburst that contributes to its far-infrared output [arXiv:astro-ph/0107148].

sources.summary: The paper reports ELAISP90 J164010+410502 as a hyperluminous infrared, broad-line radio-quiet quasar with starburst contribution, no lensing evidence, and soft X-ray properties resembling face-on Seyfert-like HyLIGs.

quotes.quote: ELAISP90 J164010+410502 ELAISP90 J164010+410502 ELAIS J1640+41 ELAIS J1640+41 ISO We report the discovery of the first hyperluminous infrared galaxy (HyLIG) in the course of the European Large Area Survey (ELAIS). This object has been detected by at 6.7, 15, and 90 , and is found to be a broad-line, radio-quiet quasar at a redshift: z = 1.099. From a detailed multi-component model fit of the spectral energy distribution, we derive a total IR luminosity: L_ IR (1-1000 m) 1.0 10 ^ 13 h_ 65 ^ 2 L _ ( q_0 = 0.5), and discuss the possible existence of a starburst contributing to the far-IR output.

Observations to date present no evidence for lens magnification. This galaxy is one of the very few HyLIGs with an X-ray detection. On the basis of its soft X-ray properties, we suggest that this broad-line object may be the face-on analogue of narrow-line, Seyfert-like HyLIGs.

The only obvious optical counterpart to the sources is found to be a point-like object at: = 16 ^ h 40 ^ m 10.16 ^ s and = +41 ^ 05 ' 22.3 '' (J2000). This source is, within the uncertainties, spatially coincident with an optically-selected quasar at a quoted redshift of 1.097 ( [ CCS88 ] 163831.4+411107; Crampton 1988).

gmw_00001628.name: NVSS J210626-314003
gmw_00001628.overview: NVSS J210626-314003 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.104 [arXiv:1506.03725]. The galaxy features extended gas that is misaligned from its radio jet axis, and its stellar component shows characteristics of a cluster central galaxy [arXiv:1506.03725].

sources.summary: NVSS J210626-314003 is a z=2.10 radio galaxy with extended gas misaligned from its jet, quiescent outer kinematics, weak environmental overdensity, and extended early-type-like stellar structure.

quotes.quote: The SINFONI maps of NVSS J210626-314003 and TXS 2353-003 are shown in Figs. fig:SINFONIobsTXS2355 and fig:SINFONIobsNVSS2106 , respectively. In NVSS J210626 314003 at z = 2.104, [ OIII ] 4959,5007 and H fall into the H band, and H , [ NII ] 6548,6583 , and [ SII ] 6716,6731 fall into the K band, both of which we cover with our data cubes.

Line widths in the extended gas of NVSS J210626-314003 and TXS 2353-003 are FWHM = 300-400 km s ^ 1 (Figs. fig:SINFONIobsTXS2355 and fig:SINFONIobsNVSS2106 ), more akin to those observed in a quasar illumination cone at z 2 lehnert09 and Ly blobs without a powerful central radio source e.g., wilman05,overzier13 , than to powerful radio galaxies, including the central regions of our two sources here. A marked difference to other HzRGs with similar data is that most of the emitting gas is at large position angles from the radio jet axis. Typically, the jet axis and the semi-major axis of the gas are aligned within about 20 ^ 30 ^ (Fig. fig:illustrationParticularityMisaligned , see also Collet et al. 2015, Nesvadba et al. 2015 in prep.).

NVSS J210626-314003, however, does not stand out as a source with a particularly dense environment of IRAC-selected sources in the wylezalek13 sample, a 1.2 excess. Unfortunately, the observing program committee of ESO only granted us narrowband imaging of TXS 2353-003, not of NVSS J210626-314003, so that we were unable to do a narrowband search around this galaxy. We note, however, that selecting a galaxy based on its bright radio emission already leads to significant biases toward galaxies in dense environments e.g., Best2000, ramos13, hatch14 , and we argue in the following that the stellar component of NVSS J210626-314003 itself shows some of the characteristic signatures of cluster central galaxies.

gmw_00001629.name: TXS 2353-003
gmw_00001629.overview: TXS 2353-003 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 1.49 [arXiv:1506.03725]. It features extended warm ionized gas that is misaligned with its radio jets [arXiv:1506.03725], and it is surrounded by a high overdensity of galaxies [arXiv:1506.03725].

sources.summary: TXS 2353-003 is a z=1.49 radio galaxy with misaligned extended ionized gas, an AGN/shock-like line ratio, spatially resolved morphology, and a strong overdensity of H emitters.

quotes.quote: Received ; in original form We report the detection of extended warm ionized gas in two powerful high-redshift radio galaxies, NVSS J210626-314003 at z = 2.10 and TXS 2353-003 at z = 1.49, that does not appear to be associated with the radio jets. This is contrary to what would be expected from the alignment effect, a characteristic feature of distant, powerful radio galaxies at z 0.6. The gas also has smaller velocity gradients and line widths than most other high-z radio galaxies with similar data.

In TXS 2353-003, we only have [ NII ] and H measured with a fairly high ratio of [ NII ] /H =0.7 in the extended gas (Fig. fig:TXS2355spectra ), which alone is sufficient to safely attribute the line emission to either shocks or photoionization from the AGN (Fig. fig:bpt ).

It can therefore only demonstrate the presence, but not the absence of such an overdensity of galaxies around a given radio galaxy. wylezalek13 show that TXS 2353-003 is surrounded by one of the highest overdensities of IRAC-selected sources, with an excess of >5 , and consistent with the results of our narrowband search presented in sec:environment .

gmw_00001630.name: B0605 085
gmw_00001630.overview: B0605 085 is a bright radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1007.0989] that exhibits periodic flux-density variability on a timescale of approximately eight years [arXiv:1007.0989]. The source also displays intranight optical variability [arXiv:1812.10614] and a helical pattern in its parsec-scale jet components [arXiv:1007.0989].

sources.summary: The paper describes B0605 085 as a bright radio quasar with periodic flux-density variability and helical jet-component behavior on roughly eight-year timescales.

quotes.quote: 77, D-50937 K o ln, Germany The quasar B0605 085 (OH 010) shows a hint for probable periodical variability in the radio total flux-density light curves. We study the possible periodicity of B0605 085 in the total flux-density, spectra and opacity changes in order to compare it with jet kinematics on parsec scales.

Our analysis of the jet kinematics in B0605 085 shows: We find strong evidence for a period of the order of 8 years ( 7.9 0.5 years when averaged over frequencies) in the total flux-density light curves at 4.8 GHz, 8 GHz and 14.5 GHz, which was observed for four cycles. The measurement of frequency-dependent time delays for the brightest outbursts in 1988, and 1995-1996 and the visual analysis for the 1973 flare shows that these flares appeared almost simultaneously at all frequencies. The spectral analysis of the flares shows that the main flares in 1973, 1988, 1995-1996 have a flat spectrum, whereas the flare in 1981 has a steep spectrum, what suggests that the four cycles of the 8-year period have different spectral properties. The average instantaneous speeds of the jet components reveal a helical pattern along the jet axis with a characteristic scale of 3 mas. This scale corresponds to a time scale of about 7.7 years, which is similar to the periodicity timescale found in the total flux-densities.

sources.summary: The source was observed twice; significant INOV and a very fast small flare appeared only in the later observation, despite no significant gamma-ray variability.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0608.0 0835: This source was observed twice, first on 2015 March 7 and then later on 2016 January 3 (Figure fig_inov1 ). Both C and F tests have confirmed the absence of flux variations on the former epoch but a significant INOV of the order of 0.1 mag is noticed during the later observations. Along with this, an extremely fast ( <10 minutes), small amplitude flare is also noticed at the beginning of the observation. To our knowledge, this is the first report of the detection of INOV from this object. The interesting fact to note is that during both the observing epochs, the source has not shown any significant ray variability (see the top panel of Figure fig_gamma1 ).

gmw_00001631.name: DES J0320 35
gmw_00001631.overview: DES J0320 35 is a radio-loud quasar located at a high redshift [arXiv:2212.06168]. Spectroscopic observations confirmed its high-redshift nature, yielding a best-fit redshift of approximately 6.13 based on a drop in its continuum spectrum [arXiv:2212.06168].

sources.summary: The paper identifies DES J0320 35 as a z>6 radio-loud QSO candidate confirmed by Gemini-South spectroscopy, with a high continuum drop and best-fit redshift near 6.13.

quotes.quote: From this sample, we have already identified two new RL QSOs at z>6 , DES J032021.431 352104.11 ( z=6.13 ; DES J0320 35 hereafter) and DES J032214.541 184118.15 ( z=6.09 ; DES J0322 18 hereafter).

While the spectroscopic observations of the majority of the sample are currently ongoing, we confirmed the high- z nature of two candidates: DES J0320 35 and DES J0322 18. These two sources were selected as the first targets to be observed because they had the highest dropout in our sample ( i-z>3 , see Fig. fig:colour_cuts ) as well as a further radio association ( 0.5 with S/N >3 ) in the quick-look images of VLASS at 3 GHz. In the left panels of Fig. fig:opt_images , we report the optical (DES, orange cross) and radio positions (RACS, dashed red circle corresponding to the radio positional uncertainty of each source) as well as the VLASS contours overlaid on the optical images in the z_ DES filter of both QSOs. Spectroscopic confirmation and optical/NIR properties We confirmed both candidates to be high- z QSOs with the Gemini-South telescope, DES J0320 35 on 2021 August 09 and DES J0322 18 on 2021 October 14-15 (program ID: GS-2021A-DD-112; P.I.

In both cases, the presence of a high drop in the continuum spectrum at about 8700 confirms the high- z nature of the candidates. However, due to the lack of evident spectral lines in the observed range, we used the observed wavelength of the drop to estimate the best-fit redshift for both sources. In particular, we considered the composite spectrum derived by from a sample of 16 QSOs at 5.6<z<6.5 with a weak Ly emission line (rest-frame equivalent width, REW, <15.4 ) and, after smoothing it to a 20 resolution, we used it to perform a fit to the observed spectrum, also smoothed to 20 , in the wavelength range 8000--9500 , where the only free parameters were the redshift and the normalisation. The best-fit redshift values we obtained are the following: z=6.13 0.05 for DES J0320 35 and z=6.09 0.05 for DES J0320 18.

gmw_00001632.name: DES J0322 18
gmw_00001632.overview: DES J0322 18 is a radio-loud quasar located at a high redshift [arXiv:2212.06168]. Spectroscopic observations confirmed its high-redshift nature, yielding a best-fit redshift of approximately 6.09 based on a high drop in its continuum spectrum [arXiv:2212.06168].

sources.summary: The paper identifies DES J0322 18 as a z>6 radio-loud QSO candidate confirmed by Gemini-South spectroscopy, with a high continuum drop and best-fit redshift near 6.09.

quotes.quote: From this sample, we have already identified two new RL QSOs at z>6 , DES J032021.431 352104.11 ( z=6.13 ; DES J0320 35 hereafter) and DES J032214.541 184118.15 ( z=6.09 ; DES J0322 18 hereafter).

While the spectroscopic observations of the majority of the sample are currently ongoing, we confirmed the high- z nature of two candidates: DES J0320 35 and DES J0322 18. These two sources were selected as the first targets to be observed because they had the highest dropout in our sample ( i-z>3 , see Fig. fig:colour_cuts ) as well as a further radio association ( 0.5 with S/N >3 ) in the quick-look images of VLASS at 3 GHz. In the left panels of Fig. fig:opt_images , we report the optical (DES, orange cross) and radio positions (RACS, dashed red circle corresponding to the radio positional uncertainty of each source) as well as the VLASS contours overlaid on the optical images in the z_ DES filter of both QSOs. Spectroscopic confirmation and optical/NIR properties We confirmed both candidates to be high- z QSOs with the Gemini-South telescope, DES J0320 35 on 2021 August 09 and DES J0322 18 on 2021 October 14-15 (program ID: GS-2021A-DD-112; P.I.

In both cases, the presence of a high drop in the continuum spectrum at about 8700 confirms the high- z nature of the candidates. However, due to the lack of evident spectral lines in the observed range, we used the observed wavelength of the drop to estimate the best-fit redshift for both sources. In particular, we considered the composite spectrum derived by from a sample of 16 QSOs at 5.6<z<6.5 with a weak Ly emission line (rest-frame equivalent width, REW, <15.4 ) and, after smoothing it to a 20 resolution, we used it to perform a fit to the observed spectrum, also smoothed to 20 , in the wavelength range 8000--9500 , where the only free parameters were the redshift and the normalisation. The best-fit redshift values we obtained are the following: z=6.13 0.05 for DES J0320 35 and z=6.09 0.05 for DES J0320 18.

gmw_00001633.name: MS1512-cB58
gmw_00001633.overview: MS1512-cB58 is a young, starbursting Lyman-break galaxy at a redshift of approximately 2.73 [arXiv:1110.2833, arXiv:0801.1187]. It is strongly gravitationally lensed and magnified by a factor of 30 by a foreground galaxy cluster [arXiv:1908.06832, arXiv:astro-ph/0002508].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes MS1512-cB58 as a well-known, strongly lensed z=2.78 galaxy, deblending Herschel photometry and fitting its optical-to-submillimeter SED.

quotes.quote: For comparison, our sample includes two previously well-studied lensed galaxies, MS1512-cB58 and the Cosmic Eye, for which we also provide updated Herschel measurements. We perform SED-fits of the full photometry of each object as well for the optical and infrared parts separately, exploring various star formation histories, using different extinction laws, and exploring the impact of nebular emission.

cB58 is a well-known very strongly lensed 30 1998MNRAS.298..945S galaxy at z=2.78 discovered by 1996AJ....111.1783Y . We use the CFHT and Spitzer optical to MIR photometry provided by and together with the submm/mm detections of and . Spectroscopy of this source is described e.g. \ in and in .

MS1512-cB58 is very close the cluster cD galaxy, which also shines in the infrared. Fortunately, its redshift is known spectroscopically to be z=0.372 . We can, therefore, extrapolate its flux at 250 m and remove it from the image, before solving for the flux of cB58 itself, in exactly the same way as we proceeded to deblend the Cosmic Eye with its closest neighbour.

sources.summary: The paper summarizes cB58 as a z=2.7276 lensed Lyman-break galaxy with large magnification, high star-formation estimates, measured mass, radius, and emission/absorption abundances.

quotes.quote: MS 1512-cB58 (hereafter, cB58) is a lensed LBG first discovered by Yee96 , with redshift z=2.7276 Pettini02 . It is magnified by a factor 30 by the cluster MS 1512+36 at z=0.37 Pettini00 . Several SFR estimates were performed for this object in the past years, leading to different results depending on the adopted SFR estimator (e.g. IR, H , UV). The available values span the range 25- 150 M_ yr^ 1 Siana08 . As noted by Siana08 , however, the highest SFR values might represent upper limits due to overestimated extinction corretions.

The estimated baryonic mass is of 10^ 10 M_ ( Pettini00,Baker04 ), whereas the effective radius is R_ eff 2 kpc Seitz98 . The observed abundances in Table t:abundance_data are from Teplitz00 and Pettini02 . The 12+ log(O/H) and log (N/O) abundances were estimated by Teplitz00 from interstellar emission lines.

sources.summary: The paper models the lensed Lyman-break galaxy cB58, comparing its size, star-formation age, dust emission, SED peak, UV field, and submillimetre observations to young-galaxy dust models.

quotes.quote: The half-light radius of cB58 is estimated to be r_ G 1.4h^ 1 kpc from a detailed image reconstruction Throughout this paper, we adopt a cosmology with (h, _0, _0)=(0.7,0.3,0.7) , where h H_0/100. . From spectroscopic observations of nebular lines, the SFR of cB58 is estimated to be 10 - 20h^ 2 M_ yr ^ 1 pettini00 . The age of major star formation in cB58 was estimated to be t_ SF 35 Myr matteucci02 .

The apparent peak of the SED is located at a wavelength shorter than 100 m at the restframe of cB58. Actually, the dust temperature is higher than that of the local ( z 0 ) sample of normal galaxies observed by SCUBA, but comparable to the most intense dusty starbursts dunne01 . It is mainly because of an intense UV radiation field of cB58 and low dust opacity. The mean dust temperature of the power-law dust model is lower than that of the single-sized dust model, but the superposition makes the submm part of its final SED similar to that of a hot dust emission. We find that both models predict slightly lower flux densities than observed.

sources.summary: The paper treats MS1512-cB58 as a bright strongly lensed high-redshift galaxy whose SED and spectra indicate a young, lightly reddened, actively star-forming stellar population.

quotes.quote: We present a comprehensive analysis of the rest-frame UV to near-IR spectral energy distributions and rest-frame optical spectra of four of the brightest gravitationally lensed galaxies in the literature: RCSGA 032727-132609 at z=1.70 , MS1512-cB58 at z=2.73 , SGAS J152745.1+065219 at z=2.76 and SGAS J122651.3+215220 at z=2.92 . This includes new Spitzer imaging for RCSGA0327 as well as new spectra, near-IR imaging and Spitzer imaging for SGAS1527 and SGAS1226. Lensing magnifications of 3-4 magnitudes allow a detailed study of the stellar populations and physical conditions.

We compare star formation rates as measured from the SED fit, the H and [ O II ] 3727 emission lines, and the UV+IR bolometric luminosity where 24 m photometry is available. The SFR estimate from the SED fit is consistently higher than the other indicators, which suggests that the Calzetti dust extinction law used in the SED fitting is too flat for young star-forming galaxies at z 2 . Our analysis finds similar stellar population parameters for all four lensed galaxies: stellar masses 3-7 10^9 M _ , young ages 100 Myr, little dust content E(B-V) =0.10-0.25, and star formation rates around 20-100 M _ yr ^ 1 . Compared to typical values for the galaxy population at z 2 , this suggests we are looking at newly formed, starbursting systems that have only recently started the build-up of stellar mass.

sources.summary: The paper detects 1.2 mm dust continuum from the lensed Lyman-break galaxy cB58 and argues that its apparent far-IR deficit mainly reflects uncertainty in its UV spectral slope.

quotes.quote: We detect 1.2 mm continuum emission from dust in the gravitationally lensed Lyman break galaxy MS 1512+36-cB58 . Our detected flux is surprisingly low: relative to local starburst galaxies, cB58 appears to produce somewhat less far-IR emission than its UV reddening predicts.

The limitations of these studies motivated us to observe the gravitationally lensed system MS 1512+36-cB58 (hereafter cB58 : Yee et al. ). Thanks to magnification by the z = 0.37 cluster MS 1512+36 , this z = 2.7 LBG was already well-studied in the rest UV and optical (e.g., Ellingson et al. ; Pettini et al. ; Teplitz et al. ), and promised to be unusually detectable at longer wavelengths. From 3--7 March 2000, we observed cB58 in on-off photometric mode with the 37-element Max-Planck Millimeter Bolometer (MAMBO) array (Kreysa et al. ) at the IRAM 30m telescope.

The most straightforward conclusion about cB58 is that its offset from the local relation in Figure fig:meur has more to do with than with F_ FIR /F_ 1600 - i.e., that the local starburst analogy holds more robustly for all other properties of cB58 than it does for the UV spectral slope. Observational systematics may be partly responsible: a spectroscopic can be artificially reddened by differential refraction, while photometry can mislead due to intergalactic absorption-- as in the case, perhaps, of the g band magnitude for cB58 used by Ellingson et al. ( ) to derive E_s(B-V) 0.3 .

sources.summary: The paper treats cB58 as a uniquely bright, lensed Lyman-break galaxy observed with VLT/UVES, using its sight line to measure excess Lyman-forest absorption against QSO sight-line expectations.

quotes.quote: A unique chance has been offered to us by VLT/UVES observations of the LBG galaxy cB58. This galaxy at z=2.724 is gravitationally magnified 50 times by an intervening galaxy cluster at z=0.37 and its optical apparent magnitude is m_V=20.64 . This is by far the brightest LBG known. We have for the first time explored the forest of a high redshift galaxy and compared the observed properties with those shown by QSO sight lines.

We found in cB58 an excess of absorption at a distance from cB58 ( z=2.724 ) of about 150 h_ 65 ^ 1 Mpc comoving ( z 2.61 ). This has been quantified by both measuring the mean flux depression D_A due to HI absorption and the number density of clouds with N_ HI > 10^ 14 atoms 2. The deviation is in both cases significant at the 3 level.

sources.summary: The paper models MS 1512--cB58 as a strongly lensed, high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy whose dust, HI column, and radiative transfer explain its observed line profile.

quotes.quote: The observed \ profile of MS 1512--cB58 is reproduced for the first time taking radiation transfer and all observational constraints into account. The observed absorption profile is found to result naturally from the observed amount of dust and the relatively high HI column density . Radiation transfer effects and suppresion by dust transform a strong intrinsic \ emission with EW( ) 60 \ into the observed faint superposed \ emission peak.

Discovered serendipitously by Yee96 , the Lyman Break Galaxy (LBG) MS 1512--cB58 (hereafter cB58) located at z 2.73 is strongly gravitationally magnified by the foreground cluster MS 1512+36. It is by far the best studied LBG, which has been observed at many wavelengths see Elli96,Fray97,Bech97,Naka97,Pett00,Tepl00,Bake01,Sawi01, Sava02,Pett02,Bake04,Tepl04 . In particular, rest-frame UV Pett00,Pett02 , optical Tepl00,Tepl04 , and radio Bake01, Bake04 observations have allowed to draw a fairly global picture of this star-forming galaxy and its interstellar medium (ISM): it is young ( 50--300 Myr), massive ( 10^ 10 ), with a sub-solar metallicity ( Z 2/5 ), a star formation rate (SFR 24 ) typical of LBGs, and it is surrounded by outflowing material at 250 .

sources.summary: The paper observes cB58 with SCUBA, detects it at 850 microns, and finds UV-based FIR predictions overestimate its submillimeter emission by a highly significant factor of 14.

quotes.quote: We have used SCUBA on the JCMT to observe two strongly lensed LBGs: the object cB58 at z=2.72 lensed by the cluster MS 1512 + 36 and the object G1 at z=4.92 lensed by the cluster MS 1358 + 62 . Rest-frame UV colors indicate significant reddening in both of these objects. For both galaxies, the method of predicting the FIR emission based on the UV properties implies strong 850 emission ( tab.results ).

The object cB58 is detected at the 4.7 level; the object G1 was not detected. In both cases the procedure of predicting the submillimeter flux from the observed color and magnitude in the rest-frame UV overpredicts the submillimeter emission. The magnitude of the discrepancies is illustrated in fig.beta . For G1 the discrepancy is not significant, given the scatter in the FIR/UV relation, but the factor 14 discrepancy for cB58 is highly significant.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1512-cB58 as a lensed z=2.723 star-forming galaxy with unusually informative UV spectrum, red continuum slope, and velocity dispersion consistent with other z~3 systems.

quotes.quote: The best case is the z = 2.723 galaxy 1512-cB58 . This spectrum (reproduced in Figure 1) is of comparable, or better, quality than HST spectra of local starburst galaxies and is a veritable mine of information for detailed studies of the physical conditions in high redshift galaxies. It now seems highly likely that this object, which is 4 magnitudes brighter than the typical z 3 galaxy in our sample, is not extraordinarily luminous but gravitationally lensed and therefore presumably provides us with an unusually clear view of a `normal' galaxy at high redshift.

In contrast, the galaxies we observe generally have UV spectral slopes between 0 and +1.5 ; the spectrum of cB58 reproduced in Figure 1, for example, can be clearly seen to be redder than flat spectrum and has = +1.3 .

A similar velocity dispersion is also found in the other two cases, 0000-D6 and cB58. (Incidentally, the fact that cB58 shows the same velocity dispersion as the other two z 3 galaxies, even though it is 10 times brighter, is another indication of its gravitationally lensed nature).

sources.summary: The review highlights 1512-cB58/cB58 as a z=2.723 high-redshift galaxy with unusually high-quality spectra, likely gravitationally lensed, reddened in the UV, and showing velocity dispersion similar to other z~3 galaxies.

quotes.quote: The best case is the z = 2.723 galaxy 1512-cB58 (Yee et al. 1996). This spectrum (reproduced in Figure 5) is of comparable quality with the best HST spectra of local starburst galaxies and is a veritable mine of information. It now seems highly likely that cB58, which is 4 magnitudes brighter than the typical z = 3 galaxy in our survey, is not extraordinarily luminous but gravitationally lensed (Williams Lewis 1997; Seitz et al. 1997) and therefore presumably provides us with an unusually clear view of a normal galaxy at high redshift.

In contrast, the galaxies we observe generally have UV spectral slopes between 0 and +1.5 ; the spectrum of cB58 reproduced in Figure 5, for example, can be clearly seen to be redder than flat spectrum and has = +1.3 .

A similar velocity dispersion is also found in the other two cases, 0000-D6 and cB58. (Incidentally, the fact that cB58 shows the same velocity dispersion as the other two z 3 galaxies, even though it is 10 times brighter, is another indication of its gravitationally lensed nature).

sources.summary: The paper compares cB58 reductions with Siana et al., attributes PAH differences to continuum treatment, and reports upper limits for both Pa and Br.

quotes.quote: For the two IRS spectra from the literature, the Clone and cB58, our reduction generally agrees with those published previously by Fadely10 and Siana08 , respectively. Small differences are noted in the PAH line fluxes due to the way that the continuum for the fit is defined. PAHFIT uses multi-component dust features to represent the continuum, while a single-component power-law is used by Fadely et al. and Siana et al.

The formal Pa line fit for cB58 yields a flux of (-0.2 2.3) 10^ 16 erg/s/cm ^2 , from which we determined the Pa flux limit to be < 2.3 10 ^ 16 erg/s/cm ^2 , which agrees with the non-detection reported by Siana08 , < 6 10^ 16 erg/s/cm ^2 . The formal Br line fit yields a line flux of (-0.4 1.3) 10^ 16 erg/s/cm ^2 , thus we report a limit of < 1.3 10^ 16 erg/s/cm ^2 .

sources.summary: The paper describes MS1512--cB58 as a gravitationally lensed, extensively studied Lyman-break galaxy whose spectra probe physical properties, kinematics, chemical abundances, and subsolar metallicity.

quotes.quote: Thanks to gravitational lensing by the foreground cluster MS1512+36 at z=0.373 , MS1512--cB58 (or cB58 for short) is the brightest Lyman break galaxy known and thus one of the most extensively studied. Following its spectroscopic confirmation as a distant galaxy by (the systemic redshift is z_ sys = 2.7276 ), analyzed spectra obtained with the Low Resolution Imaging Spectrometer (LRIS) on the Keck telescope with a view to probing the galaxy's physical and chemical properties. A more detailed study of the kinematics and chemical abundances of the interstellar gas in cB58 was made possible by high resolution and high S/N ratio observations performed with ESI on Keck pettini02 .

Among the five metallicities considered, Z = 1 and Z = 0.4 are those which most closely match the observations in both the 1425 and 1978 regions (giving comparable rms residuals between observed and model spectra). A metallicity between \ and 0.4 is in good agreement with other measures.

sources.summary: The paper uses the lensed galaxy MS 1512-cB58 as a comparison object, citing its 1425-index metallicity and unusually strong interstellar absorption-line equivalent widths.

quotes.quote: The 1425 index has already been applied successfully to the lensed galaxy MS 1512-cB58 ( z = 2.73 ) by Leitherer et al. ( ) and R04 and to Q1307-BM1163 ( z = 1.41 ) by Steidel et al. ( ) and R04. The resulting metallicities were, respectively, 0.25 Z _ and 0.8 Z _ .

On the other hand, 1.72 is only 60 of the corresponding value ( 2.90 ) for MS 1512-cB58 (cf. Pettini et al. ). Similar ratios between our sample and cB58 are also found for the weak LIS lines and for the higher ionization lines, such as Si III and Si IV. Although, as shown by Noll et al. ( ), the LIS-EW _ 0 decreases with increasing redshift and increasing Ly emission, the difference of the LIS strength between our spectra and MS 1512-cB58 can only in part be explained by the higher mean redshift and stronger mean Ly emission of our galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper includes cB58 as a well-known lensed z=2.729 star-forming galaxy, using EVLA and PdBI CO measurements to infer a lensing-corrected molecular gas fraction.

quotes.quote: We adopt for these two strongly-lensed galaxies at z_ H = 2.729 and 3.0733, respectively, the CO(1--0) integrated fluxes obtained with the Expanded Very Large Array (EVLA) by riechers10 .

Detections of the CO(3--2) line with the PdBI and measurements of their respective integrated fluxes were also reported by baker04 in cB58 and coppin07 in the Eye. Those are used in Sect. sect:luminosity-corrections to study the CO luminosity correction factors of star-forming galaxies at z>1 .
Combined with our updated stellar masses from sklias14 , we infer lensing-corrected molecular gas fractions f_ gas = 0.41 in cB58 and f_ gas = 0.18 in the Eye.

sources.summary: The paper presents near-infrared spectroscopy of the gravitationally lensed z=2.72 starburst MS1512-cB58, describing its lensed-arc morphology, magnification, optical spectrum, redshift, star-formation rate, mass, and abundances.

quotes.quote: MS1512-cB58 is a gravitationally lensed starburst galaxy at z=2.72. It was discovered serendipitously, within the field of the z=0.37 galaxy cluster MS1512+36, in spectra obtained for the CNOC survey (Yee, Ellingson, Carlberg 1996). MS1512-cB58 is clearly extended with the morphology of a lensed arc (Williams Lewis, 1997; Seitz et al., 1998). It has an ultraviolet spectrum representative of LBGs but due to a factor of 30 magnification (Seitz et al. 1998) it is orders of magnitude brighter than any other object of its kind (V=20.6, =17.8; Ellingson et al. 1996, hereafter E96).

sources.summary: The paper uses its chemical evolution model to constrain MS1512-cB58, describing it as a young Lyman break galaxy whose abundances fit an early elliptical or bulge phase.

quotes.quote: The age of the galaxy MS1512-cB58 We can constrain the age of the Lyman break galaxy MS1512-cB58 (showing that its chemical properties are consistent with those of an elliptical galaxy or a bulge in the early phases of its life, Matteucci Pipino 2002). In particular, we find that this age should be 30 Myr by comparing our model predictions on the chemical abundances with the observations of Pettini et al. (2002). In the first figure we show the prediction for [ X/Fe ] vs. time (left panel), assuming that roughly half of the iron is hidden in dust grains, compared to the observations (showed as horizontal lines) with their errors. The right panel allows us to make a stronger conclusion on the age, because neither O nor N are dust depleted.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes the bright z=2.72 LBG MS 1512--cB58 with curve-of-growth methods, using reported equivalent widths to estimate ISM metal column densities and account for contaminated Fe II lines.

quotes.quote: For a comparison of the COG with this method, we have redone the analysis for cB58 using the COG. The EWs used are given in the fourth column of Table 2 and are the ones reported by Pettini et al. (2002). The COG for cB58 is shown in Fig. 6 and the column densities are listed in Table 3. The EWs of two of the four Fe 2 lines, Fe 2 2374,2382 , have errors larger than 0.07 and 0.05  for contamination by bad pixels (Pettini et al. 2002), for which we arbitrarily use =0.15 .

sources.summary: The paper revises cB58 into agreement with the Meurer/Calzetti relation, adopts its CO(3-2) flux from prior measurements, and notes discrepant literature stellar masses.

quotes.quote: We also note that while previous studies of the Cosmic Eye and cB58 suggested that these objects were better represented by an SMC extinction law than by the meurer99 relation see e.g. wuyts12 , the new Herschel data revise the IR luminosities upward and find these galaxies in agreement with the Meurer/Calzetti relation.

cB58 We adopt for this object the CO(3-2) flux of 0.37 0.08 Jy \ measured by baker04 , which itself is consistent with an upper limit previously set by frayer97 and with the VLA CO(1--0) detection by riechers10 for the value of R_ 13 we adopt for the bulk of our sample (see MH2section ).

For example there is an order of magnitude difference for cB58 between siana08 and wuyts12 , for the Eye between richard11 and sommariva12 , and for the \ between finkelstein09 and dessauges12 or richard11 .

gmw_00001634.name: F563-1
gmw_00001634.overview: F563-1 is a bulgeless, low surface brightness disk galaxy [arXiv:1707.08085] surrounded by a faint, face-on-like disk structure [arXiv:astro-ph/9605069]. It is characterized by a low central neutral hydrogen surface density [arXiv:1707.08085] and a rotation curve that rises gradually rather than remaining flat [arXiv:astro-ph/0107366].

sources.summary: The paper revises F563-1’s optical inclination, confirms a faint face-on-like disk, and finds the new inclination agrees better with H I data and prior profiles.

quotes.quote: Note on F563-1: Comparison with other data Comparison with BHB95 will show that the optical inclination of F563-1 given here is substantially different from their inclination. This is because the image in BHB95 suffered from a modest amount of stray light and it was therefore not clear whether the extended face-on like structure around F563-1 was real or not. The images obtained at the JKT show that indeed this galaxy is surrounded by a faint face-on-like disc.

The old inclination in BHB95 was determined using the inner barlike structure, yielding a value of 50 degrees. The new value of 25 degrees derived here, is in much closer agreement with the H i data. We have determined the radial profile of F563-1 from the new JKT imaging data, using the inclination and position angle given in BHB95.

sources.summary: The paper includes F563-1 in a five-galaxy LSB sample with low central surface brightness, comparable disc scales and masses, no bulge, low central H I density, and high disc stability.

quotes.quote: Our LSB sample consist of F563-1, F568-1, F568-3, F568-VI and F579-VI. They have a B-band central surface surface brightness _B (0) ranging between 23-24 mg arcsec ^ 2 , spanning exponential disc scalelength R_D between 2.8 - 5.3 kpc and comparable dynamical masses as given by the asymptotic rotational velocity V _ max 105 - 118 kms ^ 1 , except for F568-1 which has a slightly higher V _ max 142 kms ^ 1 . All our galaxies are bulgeless. Our sample LSBs have a central HI surface density a few M _ pc ^ 2 .

We find that the median value of Q _ RW ^ min , the minimum of Q _ RW over R , lies between 2.6 and 3.1 for our sample LSBs, which is higher than the median value of 1.8 0.3 for Q _ RW ^ min for a sample of high surface brightness galaxies (HSBs) as obtained in earlier studies.

sources.summary: The paper notes independent observations of F563-1 and argues its observed rotation curve disagrees with a beam-smearing-corrected model that appeared too flat.

quotes.quote: F563-1 For this galaxy independent observations are available from de Blok Bosma (2001). See Paper I for a comparison. Note that the observed curve differs significantly from the ``beam-smearing corrected'' model presented in frankvdb . The model presented there shows an almost flat rotation curve over most of the radial range, which clearly disagrees with the new data. Beam-smearing corrections are not infallible.

gmw_00001635.name: F571-8
gmw_00001635.overview: F571-8 is an edge-on low surface brightness galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9605069, arXiv:astro-ph/0107366] that features a large central bulge [arXiv:astro-ph/9605069]. Optical observations of the galaxy show no visible evidence of an equatorial dust lane [arXiv:astro-ph/9605069].

sources.summary: The paper identifies F571-8 as the sample's only edge-on LSB galaxy, derives its radial profile specially, and notes its large bulge and lack of visible dust.

quotes.quote: Notes on F571-8: an edge-on LSB galaxy This galaxy is the only edge-on galaxy in our sample. During the initial sample selection the faint edge-on disc was not noticed, and only showed up in the H i and subsequent optical observations. As the usual ellipse fitting methods and integrating in rings to produce the radial profile, do not work for edge-on galaxies, we have produced the radial profile as follows.

This galaxy is also special in the sense that it has a very significant bulge, in contrast with all other galaxies in the sample. The magnitudes of the bulge and disc components, and the total galaxy, were measured directly from the optical image by integrating the total fluxes of the components. The structural parameters were therefore not used in determining these quantities. Using these magnitudes yields a bulge-to-disc ratio for F571-8 of 2 , which is much larger than those of the other galaxies in the sample where usually B/D 0.1 . The image shows no visible evidence for dust: there is no sign of an equatorial dust lane.

sources.summary: The paper uses F571-8 to show a rotation curve poorly explained by MOND, better handled by halo models, and strongly affected by a HIDM dark-matter disk.

quotes.quote: F571-8: MOND fails to explain this rotation curve. Indeed, the lack of gas contribution at large radius does not allows to explain the slowly increasing pattern of the rotation curve. Thus, the models which add a halo are particularly efficient compared to MOND. In addition, we note a very strong contribution due to the DM disk in the HIDM gas-scaling model.

sources.summary: The paper treats F571-8 as the only edge-on Sample I galaxy, flags optical-depth and projection concerns, but notes models suggesting dust effects in edge-on LSB rotation curves are small.

quotes.quote: F571-8 This is the only edge-on galaxy in Sample I, so we are concerned about optical depth and projection effects in the optical data. (The data are not used for this galaxy.) These effects could cause us to measure the rotation velocity at a ring where the optical depth becomes unity. Recently, matthews_edgeon have used radiative transfer models to investigate optical depth effects on rotation curves in edge-on LSB galaxies, and they conclude that these effect are likely to be small due to the low dust content in LSB galaxies.

gmw_00001636.name: F583-1
gmw_00001636.overview: F583-1 is a typical low surface brightness galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9605069]. It is well resolved and possesses a dark matter halo that strongly favors a constant-density core over a cuspy profile [arXiv:astro-ph/0107366].

sources.summary: The paper derives F583-1’s luminosity and colour profiles, describing it as a typical LSB galaxy with normal colours and average B-R and B-I values.
